content: Let me start by reading the necessary files to understand my role and the project context.
I'll read the required files to understand my instructions and project context before conducting the blueprint audit.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	---
2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
16	    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
17	
18	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
19	      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
20	        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
21	        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
22	        run);
23	      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
24	        currently under active prover work;
25	      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
26	        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
27	        dropped from objectives).
28	
29	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
31	    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
32	    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's
41	    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every
42	    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete
43	    chapter outline for each. These are not informational — treat
44	    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a
45	    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter
46	    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).
47	    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase
48	    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot
49	    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the
50	    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.
51	
52	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
53	
54	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
55	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
56	
57	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
58	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
59	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
60	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
61	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
62	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
63	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
64	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
65	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
66	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
67	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
68	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
69	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
70	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
71	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
72	    blueprint depends on):
73	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
74	       same-iter fast path below).
75	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
76	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
77	       flagged.
78	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
79	       writer was asked to fix.
80	  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
81	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
82	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
83	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
84	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
85	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
86	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
87	    still fails, F stays deferred.
88	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
89	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
90	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
91	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
92	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
93	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
94	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
95	
96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
101	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
102	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
103	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
104	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
105	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
106	    in the blueprint.
107	
108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.

116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
130	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
131	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
132	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
133	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
134	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
135	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
136	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
137	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
138	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
139	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
140	
141	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
142	
143	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
144	
145	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
146	
147	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
148	
149	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
150	
151	## Always read everything
152	
153	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
154	
155	## Unstarted-phase proposals
156	
157	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
158	
159	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. For each phase row whose `Status` is not `complete` and whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
160	
161	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
162	
163	The proposal answers:
164	
165	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
166	
167	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
168	
169	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
170	
171	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
172	
173	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
174	
175	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
176	
177	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
178	
179	```
180	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
181	
182	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
183	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
184	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
185	
186	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
187	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
188	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
189	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
190	...
191	
192	**`\uses` skeleton**:
193	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
194	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
195	
196	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
197	
198	**References for writer**:
199	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
200	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
201	
202	**Subphase choices exposed**:
203	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
204	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
205	```
206	
207	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
208	
209	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
210	
211	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.
212

223	
224	## Routes
225	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
226	
227	## References
228	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
229	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>

238	## What you do
239	
240	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
241	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
242	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
243	   - Read the entire chapter.
244	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
245	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
246	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
247	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
248	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
249	6. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
250	7. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
251	8. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
252	
253	You may also use:
254	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
255	- `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" .` — quick view of the project's Lean ↔ blueprint dependency map.
256	
257	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.

259	## Report format
260	
261	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
262	
263	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**
264	
265	Concretely:
266	
267	- `### Incomplete parts`, `### Proofs lacking detail`, `### Lean difficulty quality`, `### Multi-route coverage`, `### Citation discipline` under `## Top-level summaries`: omit each sub-section whose finding list is empty. If all five are empty, omit `## Top-level summaries` entirely — the per-chapter table already encodes "everything is fine".
268	- `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals`: omit only when every phase in the strategy has adequate blueprint coverage. When present, this section is never empty — each proposal block is a concrete outline, not a one-line flag.
269	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).
270	- `## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)`: omit entirely when none. Do NOT write a section with "None" inside; the absence of the section IS the signal.
271	- `## Per-chapter` blocks for clean chapters: when a chapter is `complete: true`, `correct: true`, and `notes` is empty, render it as a single compact line — `### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex — complete + correct, no notes.` — NOT a multi-line block with `notes: -` filler. Reserve full multi-line blocks for chapters that have actual findings.
272	- `## Severity summary`: when there are zero must-fix-this-iter and zero soon-severity items, render as `Severity summary: HARD GATE CLEARS — no findings.` and skip the per-tier breakdown.
273	
274	Filling templates with hollow "(none)" / "no drift" / "OK" content per chapter is the bloat the report should not produce. A clean 11-chapter audit should be a few hundred lines, not 16K tokens.

Do not under-classify them as "informational"; the project pays in deferred parallelism every iter a phase sits without a blueprint.

5	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
6	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
7	
8	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
9	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
10	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
11	\[
12	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
13	  \qquad
14	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
15	\]
16	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
17	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
18	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
19	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
20	
21	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
22	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
23	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
24	% groups)
25	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
26	% SOURCE QUOTE:
27	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
28	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
29	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
30	% abelian groups defined by the formula
31	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
32	% \end{dfn}
33	%  [\dots]
34	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
35	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
36	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
37	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
38	% topologies  by
39	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
40	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
41	%  \end{dfn}"
42	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
43	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
44	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
45	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
46	homomorphisms.}
47	
48	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
49	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
50	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
51	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
52	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
53	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
54	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
55	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
56	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
57	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
58	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
59	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
60	structure. This is the content of
61	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
62	
63	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
64	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
65	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
66	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
67	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
68	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
69	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
70	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
71	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
72	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
73	\begin{enumerate}
74	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
75	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
76	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
77	    isomorphism classes;
78	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
79	    for \(\otimes\);
80	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
81	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
82	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
83	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
84	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
85	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
86	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
87	\end{enumerate}
88	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
89	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
90	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
91	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
92	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
93	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
94	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
95	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
96	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
97	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
98	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
99	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
100	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
101	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
102	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
103	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
104	supplied.
105	
106	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
107	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
108	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
109	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
110	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
111	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
112	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
113	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
114	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
115	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
116	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
117	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
118	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
119	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
120	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
121	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
122	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
123	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
124	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
125	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
126	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
127	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
128	avoids. The
129	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
130	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
131	The whole \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk
132	apparatus is therefore vestigial for the group law and off the critical path.
133	
134	\section{Mathlib API survey}
135	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
136	
137	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
138	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
139	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
140	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
141	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
142	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
143	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
144	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
145	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
146	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
147	source.

227	%     (J.W (A := A)).IsMonoidal :=
228	%   .mk' _ (fun f g hf hg ↦ by
229	%     simp only [hP.W_iff (A := A)] at hf hg ⊢
230	%     intro Φ
231	%     rw [Functor.Monoidal.map_tensor]
232	%     infer_instance)"
233	\textit{Source: [Mathlib],
234	\texttt{ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal\_W}, in
235	\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean}, L48--L57.}
236	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
237	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
238	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
239	\(A\).
240	
241	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
242	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
243	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
244	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
245	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
246	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
247	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
248	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
249	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
250	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
251	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
252	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
253	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
254	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
255	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
256	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), a coherent monoidal category, or the stalkwise
257	whiskering apparatus. The route~(e) instance is therefore vestigial and off the
258	critical path.
259	
260	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
261	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
262	is already present in Mathlib: the file
263	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
264	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
265	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
266	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
267	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
268	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
269	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
270	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
271	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
272	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
273	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
274	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. Because the group law
275	is realized through the direct-gluing associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
276	via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and never through
277	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), none of this stalk apparatus is on the critical path;
278	it is retained only to document the superseded route.
279	
280	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
281	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
282	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
283	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
284	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
285	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
286	independently of any upstream timing.

288	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
289	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
290	
291	\begin{definition}
292	
293	\leanok
294	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
295	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
296	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
297	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
298	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
299	  \[
300	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
301	  \]
302	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
303	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
304	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
305	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
306	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
307	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
308	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
309	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
310	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
311	  canonical compatibility
312	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
313	     \;\cong\;
314	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
315	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
316	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
317	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
318	
319	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
320	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
321	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
322	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
323	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
324	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
325	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
326	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
327	\end{definition}
328	
329	\begin{lemma}
330	
331	\leanok
332	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
333	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
334	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
335	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
336	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
337	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
338	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
339	  \[
340	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
341	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
342	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
343	  \]
344	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
345	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
346	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
347	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
348	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
349	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
350	  \[
351	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
352	    \qquad
353	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
354	  \]
355	  the associator
356	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
357	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
358	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
359	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
360	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
361	\end{lemma}
362	
363	\begin{proof}
364	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
365	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
366	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
367	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
368	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
369	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
370	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
371	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
372	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
373	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
374	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
375	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
376	\end{proof}
377	
378	\begin{remark}
379	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
380	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
381	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
382	        lem:whisker_of_W}
383	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
384	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
385	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
386	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
387	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
388	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
389	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
390	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
391	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
392	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
393	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
394	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
395	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
396	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
397	
398	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
399	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
400	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
401	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
402	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
403	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
404	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
405	  fixed-base presheaves in
406	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
407	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
408	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
409	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
410	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
411	  it.
412	
413	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
414	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
415	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
416	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
417	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
418	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
419	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
420	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
421	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
422	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
423	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
424	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
425	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
426	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
427	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
428	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
429	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
430	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
431	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
432	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
433	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
434	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
435	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
436	\end{remark}
437	
438	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
439	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
440	
441	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
442	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
443	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
444	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
445	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
446	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
447	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
448	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
449	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
450	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
451	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
452	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
453	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
454	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
455	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
456	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
457	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
458	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
459	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
460	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
461	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
462	to the off-path route~(e).
463	
464	\begin{lemma}
465	
466	\leanok
467	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
468	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
469	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
470	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
471	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
472	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
473	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
474	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
475	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
476	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
477	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
478	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
479	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
480	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
481	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
482	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
483	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
484	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
485	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
486	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
487	  \[
488	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
489	      \;\;:=\;\;
490	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
491	  \]
492	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
493	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
494	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
495	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
496	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
497	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
498	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
499	\end{lemma}
500	
501	\begin{proof}
502	
503	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
504	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
505	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
506	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
507	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
508	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
509	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
510	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
511	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
512	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
513	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
514	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
515	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
516	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
517	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
518	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
519	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
520	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
521	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
522	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
523	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
524	
525	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
526	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
527	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
528	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
529	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
530	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
531	\end{proof}
532	
533	\begin{lemma}
534	
535	\leanok
536	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
537	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
538	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
539	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
540	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
541	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
542	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
543	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
544	  morphism
545	  \[
546	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
547	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
548	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
549	  \]
550	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
551	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
552	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
553	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
554	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
555	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
556	\end{lemma}
557	
558	\begin{proof}
559	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
560	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
561	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
562	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
563	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
564	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
565	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
566	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
567	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
568	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
569	  isomorphism}
570	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
571	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
572	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
573	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
574	  therefore extension of scalars along
575	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
576	
577	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
578	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
579	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
580	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
581	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
582	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
583	  the abstract pullback functor.
584	
585	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
586	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
587	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
588	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
589	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
590	   \;\cong\;
591	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
592	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
593	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
594	  comparison
595	  \[
596	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
597	      \;\longrightarrow\;
598	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
599	      \;\otimes\;
600	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
601	  \]
602	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
603	
604	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
605	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
606	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
607	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
608	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
609	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
610	  % "\begin{lemma}
611	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
612	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
613	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
614	  % \begin{enumerate}
615	  % \item The functor
616	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
617	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
618	  % \item The functor
619	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
620	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
621	  %  [\dots]
622	  % \end{enumerate}
623	  % \end{lemma}
624	  %  [\dots]
625	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
626	  % of functors, see
627	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
628	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
629	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
630	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
631	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
632	
633	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
634	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
635	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
636	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
637	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
638	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
639	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
640	
641	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
642	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
643	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
644	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
645	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
646	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
647	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
648	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
649	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
650	  \otimes_S N)\).
651	
652	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
653	  \[
654	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
655	  \]
656	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
657	  presheaf-level adjunction
658	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
659	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
660	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
661	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
662	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
663	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
664	  composing the resulting comparison with
665	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
666	
667	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
668	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
669	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
670	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
671	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
672	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
673	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
674	  presheaf level. This residual is being constructed \emph{directly}, mirroring the
675	  existing sheaf-level template app-for-app at the presheaf level (a Mathlib-gradient
676	  build, not a deferral to an upstream PR).
677	
678	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law for
679	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N\): the associator
680	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes the comparison
681	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\) on its sole consumer
682	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, where the restriction is commuted
683	  past the \emph{sheafified} tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) \emph{before} any local
684	  trivialisation of \(M, N\) is introduced --- so the comparison is required for the
685	  global, untrivialised modules, and no free-cover specialisation can stand in for it.
686	  Consequently the H1 residual \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong
687	  \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is genuinely on the critical path, and closing it is
688	  the \emph{single open substrate obligation} of this chapter. The superseded
689	  route~(e) \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
690	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) remains named only as a last-resort fallback should the
691	  presheaf-level adjunction itself bottom out; it is not the plan.
692	\end{proof}

694	\begin{lemma}
695	[Presheaf-level pushforward adjunction and strong-monoidal restriction (H1/H2 substrate)]
696	  \label{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}
697	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans,
698	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr,
699	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj,
700	        PresheafOfModules.isIso_of_isIso_app,
701	        PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}
702	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
703	  These five presheaf-level declarations are the two ingredients on which the
704	  linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} rests; both are absent from Mathlib at
705	  the pinned commit and are supplied project-side.
706	
707	  \emph{H1 (the pushforward adjunction).} For a morphism \(\beta\) of presheaves of
708	  rings, \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\) are the
709	  presheaf-level de-sheafifications of Mathlib's sheaf-level
710	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
711	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr}\)
712	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}),
713	  reproduced app-for-app one level down at the presheaf of modules. From them
714	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) assembles the presheaf-level adjunction
715	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), the
716	  presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
717	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\). Pairing this against the
718	  existing \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\) through
719	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) yields the canonical isomorphism
720	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) of two left
721	  adjoints of the same pushforward --- exactly the residual identified in Step~3 of
722	  the proof of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
723	
724	  \emph{H2 (strong-monoidal restriction).} \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_app}\) is the
725	  sectionwise isomorphism criterion: a morphism of presheaves of modules is an
726	  isomorphism once each of its section maps is. Built on it,
727	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}\) equips the presheaf-level
728	  restriction-of-scalars functor
729	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha\) with a \emph{strong}-monoidal
730	  structure whenever the base-change comparison \(\alpha\) is \emph{sectionwise
731	  bijective} --- the presheaf-level lift of the ModuleCat-level H2 core
732	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}, assembled section-by-section via
733	  \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\). It is to be distinguished from the
734	  module-level, ring-equivalence form
735	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\) already pinned in
736	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}: the present declaration is the
737	  \emph{presheaf}-level, \emph{bijective}-comparison form, the one the substrate lift
738	  actually consumes (where the comparison map
739	  \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)
740	  morphism but is not syntactically a \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\)).
741	
742	  Together H1 and H2 are the two Mathlib-absent ingredients the linchpin needed; they
743	  are self-contained presheaf-level results and are natural upstream-PR candidates.
744	\end{lemma}
