350	\section{Representability of the Quot scheme}
351	\label{sec:quot_representable}
352	
353	\begin{theorem}\leanok
354	[Representability of the Quot functor]
355	  \label{thm:quot_representable}
356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme}
357	  
358	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Main Existence Theorems"
359	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
360	  % L2220-L2273); cf.\ Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV.
361	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
362	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi: X\to S\) a projective morphism,
363	  %  and \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\). Then for any
364	  %  coherent \({\mathcal O}_X\)-module \(E\) and any polynomial \(\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]\),
365	  %  the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) is representable
366	  %  by a projective \(S\)-scheme \(\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\)."
367	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, Theorem (Grothendieck), Theorem
368	  (Altman--Kleiman) (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV.}
369	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a projective morphism,
370	  \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent
371	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). Then the
372	  functor \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) of \cref{def:quot_functor} is
373	  representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\),
374	  equipped with a universal quotient
375	  \[
376	    q^{\mathrm{univ}} :
377	    \pi^*_{\Quot} E \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}^{\mathrm{univ}}
378	    \quad \text{on } X \times_S \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S},
379	  \]
380	  flat over \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) with constant Hilbert polynomial
381	  \(\Phi\) on every fiber. The Hilbert scheme is the special case
382	  \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\): \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} = \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)
383	  represents the functor of \(T\)-flat closed subschemes
384	  \(Y \subset X_T\) with fiberwise Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\).
385	\end{theorem}
386	
387	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: this proof is long (~5 named sub-sections in [Nitsure]
388	% \S 5). Each sub-step is recorded with its own SOURCE QUOTE in the
389	% sub-lemmas below. The structural skeleton of the proof is restated in
390	% project notation in the \begin{proof} block.
391	\begin{proof}
392	  
393	  The proof has four steps; each step is a sub-lemma in the next
394	  sub-sections.
395	  \begin{enumerate}
396	    \item \textbf{(Boundedness, \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}.)} Pick the
397	      Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity bound $m = m(\mathrm{rank}\,V,
398	      \mathrm{rank}\,W, \Phi)$ uniformly across all fibers (by Mumford's
399	      \(m\)-regularity theorem applied to \(E_s\) and to all coherent
400	      quotients of \(E_s\) with Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\)). For \(r \ge m\)
401	      and any \(T \to S\) and any \(T\)-flat quotient
402	      \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) with Hilbert polynomial
403	      \(\Phi\), the higher direct images \(R^i (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\)
404	      and \(R^i (\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\) and \(R^i (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\)
405	      vanish for \(i \ge 1\) (where \(\mathcal{G} = \ker q\)), and the
406	      direct images \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\), \((\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\),
407	      \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\) are locally free of ranks fixed by the
408	      data; the canonical maps \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \to (-)(r)\)
409	      are surjective. Cohomology-and-base-change (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology},
410	      Stacks tag 02KH) propagates these properties across \(T\)-base change.
411	    \item \textbf{(Grassmannian embedding, \cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective}.)}
412	      Reduce by \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W} to the case
413	      \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\) with \(V, W\) locally free
414	      on \(S\), \(L = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}(V)}(1)\). Fix \(r \ge m\).
415	      The Grassmannian embedding
416	      \[
417	        \alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \longrightarrow
418	        \mathrm{Grass}\!\left(
419	          W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V,\; \Phi(r)
420	        \right)
421	      \]
422	      sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\) to the locally free
423	      quotient \((\pi_T)_*(q(r)) : (\pi_T)_* E_T(r) \to (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\).
424	      This is injective on \(T\)-valued points: from the map $T \to
425	      \mathrm{Gr}_S(W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))$ one recovers
426	      the kernel-pull-back \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r) \to E_T(r)\),
427	      whose cokernel is \(q(r) : E_T(r) \to \mathcal{F}(r)\);
428	      twisting back by \(-r\) recovers \(q\).
429	    \item \textbf{(Locally closed in the Grassmannian via flattening
430	      stratification.)} Let $T_0 = \mathrm{Gr}_S(W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V,
431	      \Phi(r))$ with universal quotient
432	      \(f : (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V)_{T_0} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{J}\)
433	      and kernel \(\mathcal{K}\). Form the composite
434	      $h : \pi_{T_0}^* \mathcal{K} \hookrightarrow
435	      \pi_{T_0}^* (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V) = \pi_{T_0}^* (\pi_{T_0})_* E_{T_0}
436	      \twoheadrightarrow E_{T_0}$
437	      and let \(q := \mathrm{coker}(h) : E_{T_0} \to \mathcal{F}\). By the
438	      flattening stratification (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
439	      of \cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\)
440	      on \(X_{T_0}\), there exists a locally closed subscheme
441	      \(T_0^\Phi \subseteq T_0\) such that for any \(\phi : Y \to T_0\), the
442	      pulled-back cokernel \(\mathcal{F}_Y\) is \(Y\)-flat with fiberwise
443	      Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) iff \(\phi\) factors through \(T_0^\Phi\).
444	      The pair \((T_0^\Phi, \text{universal cokernel})\) represents
445	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\).
446	    \item \textbf{(Closed, hence projective,
447	      \cref{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}.)} The functor
448	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) satisfies the valuative criterion of
449	      properness with respect to DVRs (Exercise (7) of \S 1, proved
450	      using that flatness over a DVR is torsion-freeness). Combined
451	      with the locally closed embedding of step 3 into the projective
452	      $\mathrm{Gr}_S \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^{\Phi(r)}
453	      (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V))$, this upgrades to a \emph{closed}
454	      embedding. Hence \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is a projective \(S\)-scheme.
455	  \end{enumerate}
456	  Finally, by \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}, the general case
457	  (arbitrary \(X\) projective over \(S\) and arbitrary coherent \(E\)) follows
458	  from the case \(X = \mathbb{P}(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\): pick a closed
459	  embedding \(X \subset \mathbb{P}(V)\) and write \(E\) as a coherent quotient
460	  of \(\pi^*(W)(\nu)\) for some vector bundle \(W\) on \(S\) and integer \(\nu\);
461	  the Quot scheme of the original data is then a closed subscheme of the
462	  Quot scheme of the universal data, by the closed-embedding clause of
463	  \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}.
464	\end{proof}
465	
466	\section{Sub-lemmas of the construction}
467	\label{sec:quot_sub_lemmas}
468	
469	\begin{lemma}[Reduction to the universal case]
470	  \label{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}
471	  
472	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Reduction to the case of
473	  % \(\Quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}\)"
474	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
475	  % L2294-L2332).
476	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
477	  % "It is enough to prove Theorem REF in the special case
478	  %  that \(X = \PP(V)\) and
479	  %  \(E= \pi^*(W)\) where \(V\) and \(W\) are vector bundles on \(S\), as
480	  %  a consequence of the next lemma.
481	  %
482	  %  \begin{lemma}
483	  %  (i) Let \(\nu\) be any integer.
484	  %  Then tensoring by \(L^{\nu}\) gives an isomorphism of
485	  %  functors from \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) to
486	  %  \(\Quot_{E(\nu)/X/S}^{\Psi,L}\) where the polynomial
487	  %  \(\Psi \in \Q[\lambda]\) is defined by \(\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)\).
488	  %
489	  %  (ii) Let \(\phi : E \to G\) be a surjective homomorphism of
490	  %  coherent sheaves on \(X\). Then the corresponding natural transformation
491	  %  \(\Quot_{G/X/S}^{\Phi,L} \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\)
492	  %  is a closed embedding.
493	  %  \end{lemma}"
494	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
495	  Let \(S\), \(X\), \(L\), \(E\), \(\Phi\) be as in \cref{def:quot_functor}.
496	  \begin{enumerate}
497	    \item (Twist-shift.) For \(\nu \in \mathbb{Z}\), tensoring by
498	      \(L^{\otimes \nu}\) gives an isomorphism of functors
499	      $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \xrightarrow{\sim}
500	      \Quot^{\Psi,L}_{E(\nu)/X/S}\( with \)\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)$.
501	    \item (Surjection induces closed embedding.) Given a surjection
502	      \(\phi : E \twoheadrightarrow G\) of coherent sheaves on \(X\), the
503	      induced natural transformation
504	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{G/X/S} \to \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is a closed
505	      embedding of functors.
506	  \end{enumerate}
507	  Consequently, if \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\pi^* W / \mathbb{P}(V)/S}\) is
508	  representable for all \(V\), \(W\) vector bundles on \(S\), then so is
509	  \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) for every \(X \subset \mathbb{P}(V)\) closed and
510	  every coherent \(E\) on \(X\) that is a quotient of \(\pi^*(W)(\nu)|_X\) for
511	  some \(W\) and \(\nu\).
512	\end{lemma}
513	
514	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The statement (i) is obvious. The statement (ii) just
515	% says that given any locally noetherian scheme \(T\) and a family
516	% \(\langle \F,q\rangle \in \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(T)\), there exists
517	% a closed subscheme \(T' \subset T\) with the following universal property:
518	% for any locally noetherian scheme \(U\) and a morphism \(f: U\to T\),
519	% the pulled back homomorphism of \({\mathcal O}_{X_U}\)-modules \(q_U : E_U \to \F_U\)
520	% factors via the pulled back homomorphism \(\phi_U : E_U \to G_U\)
521	% if and only if \(U \to T\) factors via \(T'\hra T\).
522	% This is satisfied by taking \(T'\) to be the vanishing scheme
523	% for the composite homomorphism \(\ker(\phi) \hra  E \stackrel{q}{\to} \F\)
524	% of coherent sheaves on \(X_T\) (see Remark REF),
525	% which makes sense here as both \(\ker(\phi)\) and \(\F\) are coherent
526	% on \(X_T\) and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\)."
527	\begin{proof}
528	  
529	  (i) is direct from the definitions: tensoring with \(L^{\otimes \nu}\) is
530	  an autoequivalence of coherent sheaves on \(X\) (and on every \(X_T\)),
531	  preserving surjectivity, \(T\)-flatness, and properness of support, and
532	  shifting the Hilbert polynomial by \(\nu\) in the variable.
533	
534	  (ii) Given a family \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\) in
535	  \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), the locus where \(q\) factors through
536	  \(\phi : E \twoheadrightarrow G\) is cut out by the vanishing of the
537	  composite $\ker(\phi)|_{X_T} \hookrightarrow E_T \xrightarrow{q}
538	  \mathcal{F}\(. Because \)\mathcal{F}\( is \)T$-flat, the schematic image of
539	  this composite descends to a closed subscheme \(T' \subset T\) such that
540	  \(f : U \to T\) factors through \(T'\) iff \(q_U\) factors through \(\phi_U\).
541	  Hence \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{G/X/S} \hookrightarrow \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\)
542	  is a closed embedding of subfunctors.
543	\end{proof}
544	
545	\begin{lemma}[Uniform Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity bound]
546	  \label{lem:quot_boundedness}
547	  
548	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"
549	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
550	  % L2346-L2404). Builds on [Nitsure] \S 2 (Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity)
551	  % and \S 3 (semi-continuity and base-change), cited internally as
552	  % Theorem~"Mumford" and Theorem~"Base-change for flat sheaves".
553	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
554	  % "We consider the sheaf \(E = \pi^*(W)\) on \(X=\PP(V)\) where
555	  %  \(V\) is a vector bundle on \(S\),
556	  %  and take \(L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1)\).
557	  %  For any field \(k\) and a \(k\)-valued point
558	  %  \(s\) of \(S\), we have an isomorphism
559	  %  \(\PP(V)_s \simeq \PP^n_k\) where \(n=\rank(V)-1\),
560	  %  and the restricted sheaf \(E_s\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) is isomorphic to
561	  %  \(\oplus^p{\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)_s}\) where \(p=\rank(W)\).
562	  %  It follows from Theorem REF
563	  %  that given any \(\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]\), there exists an integer
564	  %  \(m\) which depends only on \(\rank(V)\), \(\rank(W)\) and \(\Phi\),
565	  %  such that for any field \(k\) and a \(k\)-valued point \(s\) of \(S\),
566	  %  the sheaf \(E_s\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) is \(m\)-regular, and for any
567	  %  coherent quotient \(q: E_s \to \F\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) with
568	  %  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\), the sheaf \(\F\) and the kernel sheaf
569	  %  \(\G\subset E_s\) of \(q\) are both \(m\)-regular."
570	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity" (FGA Explained
571	  Ch.~5).}
572	  Let \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\) with \(V\), \(W\) vector bundles on
573	  \(S\) of ranks \(n+1\), \(p\), \(L = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}(V)}(1)\), and
574	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). There exists an integer
575	  \(m = m(n, p, \Phi)\), depending only on \(n\), \(p\), and \(\Phi\), such that
576	  for every geometric point \(s\) of \(S\) and every coherent quotient
577	  \(q : E_s \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) with Hilbert polynomial
578	  \(\Phi\), the sheaves \(E_s\), \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(\ker q\) on \(X_s\) are all
579	  \(m\)-regular in the sense of Castelnuovo--Mumford. Consequently, for
580	  every \(T \to S\) and every \(T\)-flat quotient
581	  \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) on \(X_T\) with Hilbert
582	  polynomial \(\Phi\) and every \(r \ge m\), the conclusions \textbf{(*)} and
583	  \textbf{(**)} of \cite[\S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"]{nitsure-hilbert-quot}
584	  hold: the direct images \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\), \((\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\),
585	  \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\) are locally free of fixed ranks; the
586	  canonical surjections \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \twoheadrightarrow (-)(r)\)
587	  hold; and the higher direct images \(R^i (\pi_T)_* (-)(r)\) vanish for
588	  \(i \ge 1\).
589	\end{lemma}
590	
591	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see [Nitsure], \S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"
592	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L2346-L2404).
593	% The proof invokes the m-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2
594	% (Theorem "Mumford") to extract the uniform bound m, and the
595	% base-change-for-flat-sheaves theorem of [Nitsure] \S 3 (cited as
596	% "Base-change for flat sheaves") to propagate the conclusions across \(T\).
597	\begin{proof}
598	  
599	  Each geometric fiber \(E_s\) is a trivial bundle on \(\mathbb{P}^n_{\kappa(s)}\)
600	  of rank \(p\), so \(E_s\) is \(0\)-regular. The Castelnuovo--Mumford
601	  \(m\)-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2 produces, for any fixed Hilbert
602	  polynomial \(\Phi\), a uniform integer \(m = m(n, p, \Phi)\) above which
603	  every coherent quotient of \(E_s\) with Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) -- and
604	  its kernel -- is \(m\)-regular. The Castelnuovo Lemma of [Nitsure] \S 2
605	  converts \(m\)-regularity into vanishing of \(H^i((-) (r))\) for \(i \ge 1\)
606	  and \(r \ge m\), and into global generation of \(H^0((-)(r))\). To
607	  propagate the fiber-wise statement to families over \(T\), apply the
608	  flat-base-change theorem (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}, Stacks
609	  tag 02KH) along \(T \to S\): cohomology of flat families commutes with
610	  base change, so the direct images \((\pi_T)_*(-)(r)\) are locally free of
611	  the rank dictated by the fiber Hilbert polynomials, and the canonical
612	  maps \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \to (-)(r)\) inherit fiberwise
613	  surjectivity.
614	\end{proof}
615	
616	\begin{lemma}[Grassmannian embedding \(\alpha\) is injective]
617	  \label{lem:quot_alpha_injective}
618	  
619	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"
620	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
621	  % L2409-L2451).
622	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
623	  % "We now fix a positive integer \(r\) such that \(r\ge m\).
624	  %  Note that the rank of \({\pi_T}_*\F(r)\) is \(\Phi(r)\)
625	  %  and \(\pi_*E(r) = W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V\).
626	  %  Therefore the surjective homomorphism
627	  %  \({\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\)
628	  %  defines an element of the set
629	  %  \(\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V , \Phi(r))(T)\).
630	  %  We thus get a morphism of functors
631	  %  \(\)\alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to
632	  %  \Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))\(\)
633	  %  It associates to \(q: E_T \to \F\) the quotient
634	  %  \({\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\).
635	  %
636	  %  The above morphism \(\alpha\) is injective because the quotient
637	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) can be recovered from
638	  %  \({\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\)
639	  %  as follows."
640	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"
641	  (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
642	  In the universal setup \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\), fix
643	  \(r \ge m\) from \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}. The natural transformation
644	  \[
645	    \alpha :
646	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \longrightarrow
647	    \mathrm{Grass}\!\left(
648	      W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V,\; \Phi(r)
649	    \right),
650	    \qquad
651	    \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle \longmapsto
652	    \langle (\pi_T)_*\mathcal{F}(r),\, (\pi_T)_*(q(r))\rangle
653	  \]
654	  is well-defined (the target is locally free of rank \(\Phi(r)\), and the
655	  canonical isomorphism $\pi_* E(r) \cong W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S}
656	  \mathrm{Sym}^r V\( gives the source) and \emph{injective} on \)T$-points:
657	  the data of the locally free quotient at level \(r\) determines
658	  \(q : E_T \to \mathcal{F}\).
659	\end{lemma}
660	
661	\begin{proof}
662	  
663	  Suppose \(\alpha(T)\) sends both \(\langle \mathcal{F}_1, q_1\rangle\) and
664	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}_2, q_2\rangle\) to the same \(T\)-point of the
665	  Grassmannian. Then the pulled-back kernel
666	  $(\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}_i(r) \to (\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* E_T(r)
667	  = \pi_{T_0}^* (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V)_T$
668	  is the same. The diagram \textbf{(**)} of
669	  \cref{lem:quot_boundedness} gives a right-exact sequence
670	  \[
671	    (\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}_i(r) \xrightarrow{h}
672	    E_T(r) \xrightarrow{q_i(r)} \mathcal{F}_i(r) \to 0,
673	  \]
674	  so \(q_i(r)\) is recovered as the cokernel of \(h\); twisting by \(-r\)
675	  recovers \(q_i\), giving \(q_1 = q_2\) and hence
676	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}_1, q_1\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}_2, q_2\rangle\).
677	\end{proof}
678	
679	\begin{lemma}[Valuative criterion of properness for \(\Quot\)]
680	  \label{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}
681	  
682	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Valuative Criterion for Properness"
683	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
684	  % L2510-L2542). Original reference: EGA IV(2) 2.8.1, cited there.
685	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
686	  % "The functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) satisfies the following valuative
687	  %  criterion for properness over \(S\): given any discrete valuation ring
688	  %  \(R\) over \(S\) with quotient field \(K\), the restriction map
689	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R) \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)\(\)
690	  %  is bijective. This can be seen as follows.
691	  %  Given any coherent quotient
692	  %  \(q: E_K \to \F\) on \(X_R\) which defines an element \(\langle \F, q\rangle\)
693	  %  of \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)\).
694	  %  Let \(\ov{\F}\) be the image of the composite homomorphism
695	  %  \(E_R \to j_*(E_K) \to j_*\F\) where \(j : X_K \hra X_R\) is the open
696	  %  inclusion. Let \(\ov{q} : E_R\to \ov{\F}\) be the induced surjection.
697	  %  Then we leave it to the reader to verify that
698	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) is an element of \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R)\)
699	  %  which maps to \(\langle \F, q\rangle\), and is the unique such element.
700	  %  (Use the basic fact that being flat over a dvr
701	  %  is the same as being torsion-free.)"
702	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Valuative Criterion for Properness"
703	  (FGA Explained Ch.~5); EGA IV(2) 2.8.1.}
704	  The functor \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) satisfies the valuative criterion
705	  for properness over \(S\): for every discrete valuation ring \(R\) with
706	  fraction field \(K\) and every morphism \(\Spec R \to S\), the restriction
707	  map
708	  \[
709	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec R)
710	    \longrightarrow
711	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec K)
712	  \]
713	  is bijective.
714	\end{lemma}
715	
716	\begin{proof}
717	  
718	  Given a \(\Spec K\)-point \(\langle \mathcal{F}_K, q_K\rangle\) on \(X_K\),
719	  let \(j : X_K \hookrightarrow X_R\) be the open inclusion. Define
720	  \(\overline{\mathcal{F}}\) as the image of the composite
721	  \(E_R \to j_* E_K \to j_* \mathcal{F}_K\), with induced surjection
722	  \(\overline{q} : E_R \to \overline{\mathcal{F}}\). The sheaf
723	  \(\overline{\mathcal{F}}\) is torsion-free over \(R\) (being a subsheaf of
724	  \(j_* \mathcal{F}_K\)), hence \(R\)-flat (flatness over a DVR
725	  \(=\) torsion-freeness), and restricts on the generic fiber to
726	  \(\mathcal{F}_K\). The pair \((\overline{\mathcal{F}}, \overline{q})\) is
727	  the unique extension of \(\langle \mathcal{F}_K, q_K\rangle\) to a
728	  \(\Spec R\)-point (uniqueness: any other extension agrees on the dense
729	  open \(X_K \subset X_R\) and is torsion-free, hence equals the image
730	  construction). The Hilbert polynomial is constant on connected
731	  \(\Spec R\) by flatness.
732	\end{proof}
733	
734	\section{Cohomology and base change}
735	\label{sec:cohomology_base_change}
736	
737	The Quot construction uses cohomology-and-base-change in two places: the
738	boundedness step (\cref{lem:quot_boundedness}) and the Grassmannian
739	embedding (\cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective}). We record the relevant
740	statement as a named theorem so the Lean encoding can cite it directly.
741	
742	\begin{theorem}\leanok
743	[Flat base change of cohomology]
744	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChangeCohomology}
746	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology)
747	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947-L970).
748	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
749	  % "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
750	  %  \(\)
751	  %  \xymatrix{
752	  %  X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
753	  %  S' \ar[r]^g & S
754	  %  }
755	  %  \(\)
756	  %  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
757	  %  with pullback \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\).
758	  %  Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
759	  %  For any \(i \geq 0\)
760	  %  \begin{enumerate}
761	  %  \item the base change map of
762	  %  Cohomology, Lemma REF
763	  %  is an isomorphism
764	  %  \(\)
765	  %  g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
766	  %  \(\)
767	  %  \item if \(S = \Spec(A)\) and \(S' = \Spec(B)\), then
768	  %  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\).
769	  %  \end{enumerate}"
770	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology).}
771	  Let
772	  \[
773	    \begin{array}{ccc}
774	      X' & \xrightarrow{g'} & X \\
775	      \downarrow f' &  & \downarrow f \\
776	      S' & \xrightarrow{g} & S
777	    \end{array}
778	  \]
779	  be a cartesian diagram of schemes with \(g\) flat and \(f\) quasi-compact and
780	  quasi-separated. Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent
781	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module with pull-back
782	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^* \mathcal{F}\). For every \(i \ge 0\):
783	  \begin{enumerate}
784	    \item the base-change map
785	      \(g^* R^i f_* \mathcal{F} \to R^i f'_* \mathcal{F}'\) is an isomorphism;
786	    \item if \(S = \Spec A\) and \(S' = \Spec B\), then $H^i(X, \mathcal{F})
787	      \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
788	  \end{enumerate}
789	\end{theorem}

792	\label{subsec:quot_project_basechange}
793	
794	The Lean encoding of \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology} (and the
795	Quot construction's iso between pushforward and tensor-pullback for the
796	universal section pattern) relies on four project-side declarations
797	introduced in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. These
798	are typed-sorry today: each carries a substantive Lean type that

803	\begin{definition}\leanok
804	[Canonical base-change map on sheaves of modules]
805	  \label{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map}
806	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap}
807	  \uses{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
808	  Given a cartesian square of schemes
809	  \(\square = (g' : X' \to X,\ f' : X' \to S',\ f : X \to S,\ g : S' \to S)\)
810	  with \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) and \(g\) flat, the
811	  \emph{canonical base-change map} on sheaves of modules is the natural
812	  transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \longrightarrow f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\)
813	  arising from the universal property of pullback. Encoded as a morphism
814	  of \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\)-valued sheaf functors on the pulled-back open
815	  cover; this is the morphism whose iso-status discharges
816	  \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}(1) in the present
817	  finite-locally-free / quasi-coherent-of-finite-type setting.
818	\end{definition}
819	
820	\begin{theorem}\leanok
821	[Stalkwise base-change isomorphism on affines]
822	  \label{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}
823	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap_app_app_isIso}
824	  \uses{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map, thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
825	  For every affine open \(V \subseteq S'\) and every affine open
826	  \(U \subseteq X\) lying over \(V\), the local component
827	  \((\text{canonicalBaseChangeMap}\ \square).\text{app}\ \mathcal{F}.\text{app}\ U\)
828	  of \cref{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map} is an isomorphism. Proof
829	  reduces (Stacks 02KH(2)) to the affine-base case: \(H^0\) commutes with
830	  flat extension of scalars on a quasi-compact quasi-separated affine
831	  morphism.
832	\end{theorem}

834	\begin{theorem}\leanok
835	[Canonical base-change map is a sheaf isomorphism]
836	  \label{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_isIso}
837	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap_isIso}
838	  \uses{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}
839	  Globalising the stalk-wise statement
840	  \cref{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}: the natural
841	  transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \to f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\) of
842	  \cref{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map} is an isomorphism of
843	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules. This is the sheaf-level form of

845	\end{theorem}

847	\begin{definition}\leanok

865	  (Step 2, project-side typed sorry): the

944	\subsection{Iter-187 analogist-licensed named substrate gaps}
945	\label{sec:quot_analogist_gaps}
946	
947	The iter-187 \texttt{mathlib-analogist quotscheme-isbasechange-tilde}
948	verdict (see \texttt{analogies/quotscheme-isbasechange-tilde.md})
949	identified two distinct Mathlib substrate gaps at the pinned commit
950	\texttt{b80f227}. Both are pinned here as named project-side typed
951	sorries; closing either is iter-189+ sub-build work.
952	
953	\begin{lemma}
954	
955	\leanok
956	[Pullback of tilde is tilde of base change (Stacks 01HQ)]
957	  \label{lem:pullback_tildeIso}
958	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_tildeIso}
959	  \uses{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map}
960	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HQ}{01HQ}
961	  / \href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/0BJ8}{0BJ8} (pullback of
962	  quasi-coherent modules along a Spec morphism).}
963	  For a ring homomorphism \(\varphi : A \to B\) and an \(A\)-module
964	  \(M\), there is a canonical iso between the pullback of the
965	  tilde-sheaf and the tilde of the base-changed module:
966	  \[
967	    (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\, (\mathrm{Spec.map}\, \varphi)).\mathrm{obj}\,
968	      (\widetilde{M})
969	    \;\cong\;
970	    \widetilde{B \otimes_A M}.
971	  \]
972	  Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} ships
973	  \texttt{PullbackFree.lean:122 pullbackObjFreeIso} for \emph{free} sheaves
974	  only — the general-module form (Stacks 01HQ) is unowned.
975	\end{lemma}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  
93	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
94	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
95	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
96	  % "In Situation REF.
97	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
98	  %  \(\)
99	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
100	  %  \(\)
101	  %  with the following properties:
102	  %  \begin{enumerate}
103	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
104	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
105	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
106	  %  \(\)
107	  %  \xymatrix{
108	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
109	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
111	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
112	  %  }
113	  %  \(\)
114	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
115	  %  \end{enumerate}
116	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
117	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
118	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
119	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
120	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
121	  (Definition~REF). There exists a scheme
122	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
123	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
124	  \begin{enumerate}
125	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
126	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
127	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
128	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
129	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
130	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
131	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
132	  \end{enumerate}
133	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
134	  over \(X\).
135	\end{theorem}
136	
137	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
138	% Lemmas REF,
139	% REF, and
140	% REF.
141	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
142	% Lemma REF."
143	\begin{proof}
144	  
145	  
146	\leanok
147	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
148	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
149	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
150	  open-immersion transition data
151	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
152	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
153	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
154	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
155	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
156	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
157	\end{proof}

161	  \label{thm:relative_spec_univ}
162	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}

197	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
198	  Then \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents the functor

222	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
223	  % The iter-179 Block A refactor replaces the iter-176 placeholder bodies with
224	  % the Mathlib values ``(AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData _).glued`` /
225	  % ``.toBase``; iter-179 Lane B closes the Lean ``UniversalProperty`` proof
226	  % via ``isAffineHom_of_forall_exists_isAffineOpen`` +

233	  % Yoneda-bijection witness is still deferred.
234	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor \(F\) is a Zariski sheaf
235	  because morphisms of schemes and \(\mathcal{O}\)-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
236	  Choose an affine open cover \(X = \bigcup U_i\). The subfunctor
237	  \(F_i \subseteq F\) of pairs \((f, \varphi)\) with \(f(T) \subseteq U_i\) is representable by

252	[Relative spectrum over an affine base]

272	  \(R\)-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(X\)-schemes
273	  \[
274	    \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\; \Spec(A).
275	  \]
276	  In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute
277	  spectrum of the global sections.
278	\end{theorem}

288	% We have

292	% \(\)
293	% \varphi_{univ} :
294	% f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
295	% \longrightarrow
296	% \widetilde A = \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(A)}
297	% \(\)
298	% coming from the \(A\)-module map \(A \otimes_R A \to A\),

312	  \(\mathcal{A}\) gives \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
313	  (with the \(R\)-algebra structure on \(A\) inducing the algebra structure on

365	  Let \(g : T \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a quasi-coherent
366	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra. Then \(g^* \mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
367	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-algebra and there is a canonical isomorphism of \(T\)-schemes
368	  \[
369	    T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\;
370	    \underline{\Spec}_T(g^* \mathcal{A}).
371	  \]
372	\end{theorem}

374	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "A pair \((f' : T \to S', \varphi' : (f')^*\mathcal{A}' \to \mathcal{O}_T)\)
375	% is the same as a pair \((f, \varphi : f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T)\)
376	% together with a factorization of \(f\) as \(f = g \circ f'\). Namely with

380	\begin{proof}
381	  
382	  \leanok
383	  % NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-179): the iter-185 Lane D HARD-BAR
384	  % closed BOTH iter-179 Mathlib-gap sorries kernel-clean:
385	  % (i) ``Scheme.QcohAlgebra.pullback_coequifibered`` (the ``coequifibered``
386	  % field, RelativeSpec.lean L358-377) via

389	  % (ii) ``Scheme.RelativeSpec.pullback_iso_desc_isIso`` (the canonical iso body,

398	  % is functionally complete.
399	  By Theorem~REF, \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents
400	  the functor \(F\) sending an \(X\)-scheme \(h : S \to X\) to the set of
401	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra maps \(h^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_S\). A morphism
402	  \(S \to T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) over \(T\) is the same as a pair of an
403	  \(X\)-morphism \(S \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with a \(T\)-factorisation
404	  of \(S \to X\), i.e.\ a morphism \(S \to T\) whose composition with \(g\) equals
405	  \(S \to X\). Tracing through, this is a \(T\)-morphism \(f' : S \to T\) together with an
406	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra map \((f')^*(g^*\mathcal{A}) = f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_S\).

408	  \(T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to \underline{\Spec}_T(g^*\mathcal{A})\)

432	  %  \(S\)}, or simply the {\it spectrum of \(\mathcal{A}\) over \(S\)} is the scheme

453	  [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
454	  The construction \(\mathcal{A} \mapsto \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) assembles into
455	  a contravariant functor
456	  \[
457	    \underline{\Spec}_X : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AffSch}/X
458	  \]
459	  from quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras to affine \(X\)-schemes: a morphism of
460	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras \(\psi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) induces, by the universal
461	  property (Theorem~REF) applied to the pair
462	  $(\pi_\mathcal{B}, \psi \circ \pi_\mathcal{B}^*) :
463	  \pi_\mathcal{B}^*\mathcal{A} \to \pi_\mathcal{B}^*\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec\mathcal{B}}$,
464	  an \(X\)-morphism
465	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\psi) : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\).
466	  Functoriality (identity, composition) is direct from the universal property. The
467	  pushforward \(\pi_* \mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})} \cong \mathcal{A}\)
468	  exhibits \(\underline{\Spec}_X\) as an equivalence between \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}\) and
469	  the full subcategory of affine \(X\)-schemes, with inverse \(f \mapsto f_* \mathcal{O}_Y\)
470	  (Stacks lemma-spec-properties part~(3)).

473	\begin{proof}
474	  
475	  \leanok
476	  Functoriality follows directly from Theorem~REF: for
477	  \(\psi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\), the pair
478	  $(\pi_\mathcal{B} : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to X, \;
479	  \pi_\mathcal{B}^*(\psi))\( defines an \)X$-morphism
480	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) via the
481	  representing universal pair. Identity and composition follow from naturality of the
482	  representing bijection in \(\mathcal{A}\). For the equivalence claim, \(\underline{\Spec}_X\)
483	  is fully faithful because the isomorphism
484	  \(\pi_* \mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})} \cong \mathcal{A}\)
485	  (locally identifying with \(\Gamma(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)),\mathcal{O}) = \mathcal{A}(U)\)
486	  via Theorem~REF) reconstructs \(\mathcal{A}\) from
487	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\). Essential surjectivity onto affine \(X\)-schemes uses
488	  the canonical morphism \(X \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\pi_*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of Stacks
489	  lemma-canonical-morphism; on an affine \(X\)-scheme \(\pi : Y \to X\), this canonical
490	  morphism is an isomorphism (affine-locally it is the identity
491	  \(\Spec\Gamma(\pi^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_Y) \to \Spec\Gamma(\pi^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_Y)\)).
492	\end{proof}

494	\section{Lean encoding}
495	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
496	
497	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
498	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
499	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
500	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
501	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
502	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
503	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
504	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
505	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
506	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
507	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
508	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
509	(Theorem~REF) is expressed as a
510	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
511	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
512	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
513	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. The base-change isomorphism
514	(Theorem~REF) and functoriality
515	(Theorem~REF) are derived from the universal property by
516	Yoneda. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a definitional/structural
517	construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme infrastructure.
518

2362	hom; each block names the intended Lean declaration and its dependencies. The
2363	specialisation that closes \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is recorded in
2364	\cref{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}; an alternative descent-theoretic route is noted in
2365	\cref{rem:dual_via_stack}.
2366	
2367	A structural point governs the whole construction. The naive rule
2368	\(U \mapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) is \emph{contravariant} in the
2369	restriction maps of \(M\) (a restriction \(M(U) \to M(V)\) for \(V \subseteq U\)
2370	induces precomposition \(\Hom(M(V), N(V)) \to \Hom(M(U), \cdot)\) in the wrong
2371	direction), so it is \emph{not} a presheaf in the covariant sense and cannot be
2372	sheafified like the tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The remedy is
2373	the \emph{slice} formula \(U \mapsto (M|_U \to N|_U)\) --- the module of morphisms of
2374	\emph{restricted} modules over the restricted ring --- whose presheaf functoriality
2375	(restrict a global-over-\(U\) morphism to a global-over-\(V\) morphism, for
2376	\(V \subseteq U\)) \emph{is} covariant. Constructing that slice presheaf, and
2377	proving it satisfies the sheaf condition, is the substance of the build.
2378	
2379	\begin{definition}
2380	
2381	\leanok
2382	[The global-ring module on morphisms of presheaves of modules (value module)]
2383	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2384	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.homModule}
2385	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2386	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2387	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2388	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3508--L3514).
2389	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2390	  % "abelian groups. In addition, given an element
2391	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2392	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
2393	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2394	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
2395	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
2396	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
2397	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2398	  (tag area 01CM): the section ring acts on a morphism of restricted modules by
2399	  post-composition with multiplication by the section.}
2400	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a base category \(\mathcal{C}\)
2401	  that has a terminal object \(T\), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\).
2402	  The abelian group of morphisms \(M \longrightarrow N\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
2403	  carries a canonical \(R(T)\)-module structure --- the \emph{morphism module}
2404	  \(\mathtt{homModule}\,M\,N\). A global scalar \(f \in R(T)\) acts on a morphism
2405	  \(\varphi : M \to N\) by post-composition with multiplication by \(f\),
2406	  \[
2407	    f \cdot \varphi \;:=\; m_f^{N} \circ \varphi,
2408	  \]
2409	  where \(m_f^{N} : N \to N\) is the \emph{multiplication-by-\(f\)} endomorphism of
2410	  \(N\): the endomorphism that, on each object \(U \in \mathcal{C}\), is multiplication
2411	  by the restriction \(f|_U \in R(U)\) of \(f\) along the unique morphism \(U \to T\)
2412	  to the terminal object. The module axioms hold because the assignment
2413	  \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\) is a ring homomorphism \(R(T) \to \mathrm{End}(N)\) into the
2414	  endomorphism ring of \(N\) --- a product \(f \cdot g\) goes to the \emph{composite}
2415	  \(m_f^{N} \circ m_g^{N}\) of the two multiplication endomorphisms, the order in which
2416	  the factors compose being reversed by the fact that the scalar acts by
2417	  \emph{post}-composition --- and composition of morphisms is biadditive in the
2418	  preadditive category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). Distributivity and the
2419	  associativity of the action then follow from bilinearity of composition together with
2420	  the homomorphism property of \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\). This is the genuinely new core of
2421	  the construction: Mathlib supplies the \emph{fixed}-ring internal hom
2422	  \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\) over a single base ring, but nothing equipping the
2423	  morphism group with the module structure for the \emph{varying} structure presheaf at
2424	  the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
2425	\end{definition}
2426	
2427	\begin{definition}
2428	
2429	\leanok
2430	[Slice specialisation of the morphism module]
2431	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
2432	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHomObjModule}
2433	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2434	  Specialise \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} to a slice category. For an object
2435	  (open) \(U\) of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\), restricting along the forgetful functor
2436	  \(\mathtt{Over.forget}\,U : \mathcal{C}/U \to \mathcal{C}\) --- the open-immersion
2437	  restriction \(M \mapsto M|_U\) --- carries \(M, N\) to presheaves of modules
2438	  \(M|_U, N|_U\) over the restricted structure ring on the slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\). The
2439	  slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\) has the terminal object \(\mathtt{Over.mk}\,(\mathrm{id}_U)\),
2440	  at which the restricted ring takes the value \(R(U)\); hence
2441	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} makes the morphism group
2442	  \(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\) an \(R(U)\)-module, the
2443	  \(\mathtt{internalHomObjModule}\). This \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) is exactly
2444	  the value \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
2445	\end{definition}
2446	
2447	\begin{definition}
2448	
2449	\leanok
2450	[Presheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
2451	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2452	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHom}
2453	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:presheaf_internal_hom_value,
2454	        def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2455	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2456	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2457	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3500--L3524).
2458	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2459	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2460	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2461	  % Consider the rule
2462	  % $$
2463	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2464	  % $$
2465	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2466	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2467	  % abelian groups. In addition, given an element
2468	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2469	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
2470	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2471	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
2472	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
2473	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2474	  % We will denote this sheaf
2475	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})$.
2476	  % There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
2477	  % $$
2478	  % \mathcal{F}
2479	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2480	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
2481	  % \longrightarrow
2482	  % \mathcal{G}.
2483	  % $$"
2484	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2485	  (tag area 01CM).}
2486	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on \(\Opens X\) and let
2487	  \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). The \emph{presheaf internal hom}
2488	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules whose value on an open
2489	  \(U\) is the module of morphisms of restricted modules
2490	  \[
2491	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)
2492	      \;:=\;
2493	    \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\;\bigl(R(U)\bigr)\;\bigl(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\bigr),
2494	  \]
2495	  the \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear morphisms \(M|_U \to N|_U\) of presheaves of
2496	  modules on \(U\), formed via the open-immersion restriction \(U \hookrightarrow X\).
2497	  This value module is the slice specialisation
2498	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value} of the morphism module
2499	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}.
2500	
2501	  Explicitly, \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules assembled from
2502	  three pieces. \emph{(a) Value modules.} On each open \(U\) the value is the
2503	  \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} /
2504	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}. \emph{(b) Restriction maps.} For an
2505	  inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) the restriction map
2506	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \to \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V)\) is the further-restriction map
2507	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}, \(\varphi \mapsto \varphi|_V\), which
2508	  is additive and semilinear over the ring restriction \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\)
2509	  induced by \(g : V \to U\) --- the compatibility datum that the presheaf-of-modules
2510	  assembly (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.mk}\), via \(\mathtt{ofPresheaf}\)) consumes.
2511	  \emph{(c) Functoriality.} Restriction along the identity \(U \to U\) is the identity
2512	  map, and restriction along a composite \(V \xrightarrow{g} U \xrightarrow{h} W\) of
2513	  inclusions \(V \subseteq U \subseteq W\) (the morphism \(g : V \to U\) followed by
2514	  \(h : U \to W\) in \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\)) is the composite of the two
2515	  restriction maps \(\mathcal{H}om(M,N)(W) \to \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(U) \to
2516	  \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(V)\); both identity and composition are inherited from the
2517	  functoriality of further restriction, since \(\mathtt{Over.map}\) is a functor. These three pieces
2518	  make \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) a genuine, covariant presheaf of \(R\)-modules; this
2519	  assembled presheaf is the construction target \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.internalHom}\).
2520	
2521	  The slice formula is forced by contravariance: the pointwise rule
2522	  \(U \mapsto \Hom_{R(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) varies \emph{against} the restriction maps of
2523	  \(M\) and so is not a covariant presheaf, whereas the module of morphisms of
2524	  \emph{restricted} objects \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricts covariantly. Thus, unlike the
2525	  tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} --- which is covariant in the
2526	  restriction maps and sheafifies directly --- the internal hom must be built through
2527	  the slice presentation, and there is no presheaf-level dual to sheafify naively.
2528	\end{definition}
2529	
2530	\begin{lemma}
2531	
2532	\leanok
2533	[Restriction map of the presheaf internal hom]
2534	  \label{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2535	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.restrictionMap}
2536	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value, def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
2537	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2538	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2539	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3508).
2540	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2541	  % "Consider the rule
2542	  % $$
2543	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2544	  % $$
2545	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2546	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2547	  % abelian groups."
2548	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2549	  (tag area 01CM): the rule \(U \mapsto \Hom(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)\), with its
2550	  further-restriction maps, is a (pre)sheaf of abelian groups.}
2551	  Let \(g : V \to U\) be a morphism of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\) (an inclusion
2552	  \(V \subseteq U\) of opens), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). There
2553	  is a restriction map
2554	  \[
2555	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \longrightarrow \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V),
2556	    \qquad \varphi \longmapsto \varphi|_V,
2557	  \]
2558	  the \(\mathtt{restrictionMap}\), sending a morphism of restricted modules
2559	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to its further restriction \(M|_V \to N|_V\). Concretely, the
2560	  functor \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g : \mathcal{C}/V \to \mathcal{C}/U\) realises
2561	  ``restrict from \(U\) to \(V\)'', and \(\varphi|_V\) is \(\varphi\) pulled back along
2562	  it --- the further restriction of the same morphism of presheaves of modules. This
2563	  map is additive, and it is \emph{semilinear} over the ring restriction
2564	  \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\): for \(f \in R(U)\),
2565	  \[
2566	    (f \cdot \varphi)\big|_V \;=\; R(g)(f) \cdot \bigl(\varphi|_V\bigr).
2567	  \]
2568	  This semilinearity is the compatibility datum that the presheaf assembly of
2569	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} (through \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf}\) /
2570	  \(\mathtt{mk}\)) requires.
2571	\end{lemma}
2572	
2573	\begin{proof}
2574	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2575	  Further restriction along \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g\) is functorial, so it carries a
2576	  morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to a morphism \(M|_V \to N|_V\) and respects identities and
2577	  composites; in particular the induced map on morphism groups is additive. For the
2578	  semilinearity, recall that the global-scalar action of
2579	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} acts on \(\varphi\) by post-composition with
2580	  the multiplication-by-\(f\) endomorphism \(m_f^{N}\); restricting that endomorphism
2581	  from \(U\) to \(V\) is multiplication by \(f|_V = R(g)(f)\) on \(N|_V\). Since further
2582	  restriction commutes with composition of morphisms, restricting \(f \cdot \varphi =
2583	  m_f^{N} \circ \varphi\) yields \(m_{R(g)(f)}^{N|_V} \circ (\varphi|_V) = R(g)(f) \cdot
2584	  (\varphi|_V)\), which is the asserted compatibility.
2585	\end{proof}
2586	
2587	\begin{definition}
2588	
2589	\leanok
2590	[Presheaf dual against the structure presheaf]
2591	  \label{def:presheaf_dual}
2592	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dual}
2593	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2594	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as in \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}, the
2595	  \emph{presheaf dual} of \(M\) is the internal hom into the unit (structure)
2596	  presheaf,
2597	  \[
2598	    M^\vee \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om(M, R),
2599	  \]
2600	  i.e.\ \(M^\vee(U) = \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\,(R(U))\,(M|_U \to R|_U)\), the
2601	  \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\). This is the
2602	  presheaf-of-modules analogue of the linear dual
2603	  \(\mathtt{Module.Dual}\,R\,M = (M \to R)\) that serves as the \(\otimes\)-inverse of
2604	  an invertible module over a fixed ring; here the fixed-ring dual is taken
2605	  open-by-open and assembled by the slice presentation of
2606	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
2607	\end{definition}
2608	
2609	\begin{lemma}
2610	
2611	\leanok
2612	[Evaluation/contraction of the internal hom]
2613	  \label{lem:internal_hom_eval}
2614	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.internalHomEval}
2615	  % NOTE (iter-224): \texttt{PresheafOfModules.internalHomEval} is CLOSED axiom-clean
2616	  % (\(\{\texttt{propext}, \texttt{Classical.choice}, \texttt{Quot.sound}\}\)). The full
2617	  % morphism assembles the per-open contraction \texttt{internalHomEvalApp}
2618	  % (the \(R(U)\)-bilinear \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\),
2619	  % \(s \otimes \varphi \mapsto \varphi(s)\)) sectionwise; naturality follows from the
2620	  % evaluate/restrict-commutation argument in the proof body. The iter-222/223 ``whnf
2621	  % heartbeat-bomb'' obstacle was a stale Mathlib-version artifact and is gone.
2622	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2623	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2624	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2625	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3517--L3524).
2626	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2627	  % "There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
2628	  % $$
2629	  % \mathcal{F}
2630	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2631	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
2632	  % \longrightarrow
2633	  % \mathcal{G}.
2634	  % $$"
2635	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2636	  (tag area 01CM).}
2637	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as above, there is a canonical \emph{evaluation} (contraction)
2638	  morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
2639	  \[
2640	    \mathrm{ev}_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_R M^\vee \;\longrightarrow\; R,
2641	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
2642	  \]
2643	  the counit of the tensor--hom adjunction
2644	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M \otimes_R -, R) \cong \mathcal{H}om(-, M^\vee)\) specialised at
2645	  \(R\). On each open \(U\) it is the open-by-open contraction
2646	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R|_U} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R|_U\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto
2647	  \phi(s)\), and these are compatible with restriction, so they assemble to a morphism
2648	  of presheaves of modules. The tensor product is the presheaf tensor underlying
2649	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
2650	\end{lemma}
2651	
2652	\begin{proof}
2653	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2654	  On a fixed open \(U\), the value \(M^\vee(U) = (M|_U \to R|_U)\) is the module of
2655	  \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\), and the evaluation
2656	  \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\) is the standard contraction
2657	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\) bilinear over \(R(U)\), hence a
2658	  well-defined \(R(U)\)-linear map out of the tensor product. For \(V \subseteq U\)
2659	  the square relating the \(U\)- and \(V\)-contractions commutes because evaluating
2660	  then restricting equals restricting both arguments then evaluating
2661	  (\(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\)); hence the open-by-open contractions are natural
2662	  in the restriction maps and define a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules.
2663	  Concretely, the morphism is defined by specifying, on each open \(U\), the per-open
2664	  contraction \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\).
2665	  The single remaining datum is the naturality square, which reduces to the
2666	  evaluate/restrict-commutation identity \(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\) above: the
2667	  restriction \(\phi|_V\) of a functional inside the dual \(M^\vee\) is its restriction
2668	  to the open \(V\), and restriction is functorial, so evaluating the restricted
2669	  functional on the restricted section agrees with restricting the value of the
2670	  evaluation. This is the same restriction-functoriality argument already used to make
2671	  the internal-hom restriction maps functorial in
2672	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}. This is the counit of the tensor--hom
2673	  adjunction of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} specialised to the unit presheaf.
2674	\end{proof}
2675	
2676	\begin{lemma}
2677	
2678	\leanok
2679	[The internal hom is a sheaf; the sheaf-level dual]
2680	  \label{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}
2681	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual}
2682	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
2683	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2684	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2685	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3514).
2686	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2687	  % "Consider the rule
2688	  % $$
2689	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2690	  % $$
2691	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2692	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2693	  % abelian groups. ... Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
2694	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2695	  (tag area 01CM).}
2696	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), and let
2697	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with \(N\) a sheaf of modules. Then the
2698	  presheaf internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2699	  satisfies the sheaf condition: for an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\), a family of
2700	  morphisms \(M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) agreeing on the overlaps
2701	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) glues to a unique morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\), because \(N\) is a
2702	  sheaf and morphisms of sheaves of modules are determined and gluable section-wise.
2703	  Hence \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) descends to an object of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
2704	  Specialising to \(N = \mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,R\) gives the
2705	  \emph{sheaf-level dual}
2706	  \[
2707	    \mathtt{dual}\,M \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X)
2708	    \;\in\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X,
2709	  \]
2710	  and the evaluation \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval} descends to a morphism of sheaves of
2711	  modules \(M \otimes_X \mathtt{dual}\,M \to \mathcal{O}_X\).
2712	\end{lemma}
2713	
2714	\begin{proof}
2715	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
2716	  Fix an open \(U\) and an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\). A morphism
2717	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) is a section of \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) over \(U\); restricting it
2718	  to the \(U_i\) gives a compatible family. Conversely, given morphisms
2719	  \(\varphi_i : M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) with \(\varphi_i|_{U_i \cap U_j} =
2720	  \varphi_j|_{U_i \cap U_j}\), section-wise gluing in the sheaf \(N\) produces, for
2721	  each open \(W \subseteq U\) and section \(s \in M(W)\), a well-defined section of
2722	  \(N(W)\) by gluing the \(\varphi_i(s|_{W \cap U_i})\) (these agree on overlaps by
2723	  hypothesis, and \(N\) is a sheaf so the glued section is unique). The resulting
2724	  assignment is \(R(W)\)-linear and natural in \(W\), hence a morphism
2725	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricting to the \(\varphi_i\); uniqueness is the separatedness
2726	  of \(N\). This is exactly the sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\), so it is a
2727	  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Taking \(N = \mathcal{O}_X\) yields
2728	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,M\), and the evaluation morphism of \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval},
2729	  being a morphism of presheaves into the sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\), is a morphism of
2730	  the corresponding sheaves of modules.
2731	\end{proof}
2732	
2733	\begin{lemma}
2734	
2735	
2736	\leanok
2737	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with the linear dual (H2$'$)]
2738	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
2739	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv}
2740	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
2741	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M\) be an
2742	  \(S\)-module. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the formation of the
2743	  linear dual: the canonical map
2744	  \[
2745	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \to_S S\bigr)
2746	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2747	    \bigl(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \to_R R\bigr),
2748	    \qquad \varphi \;\longmapsto\; e^{-1} \circ \varphi,
2749	  \]
2750	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence, with inverse \(\psi \mapsto e \circ \psi\). On both
2751	  sides the \(R\)-module structure is the \(S\)-module structure of \(M\) pulled back
2752	  along \(e\), so that \(r \cdot m = e(r) \cdot m\): the left-hand side is the
2753	  \(S\)-linear dual \((M \to_S S)\) regarded as an \(R\)-module by restriction, and the
2754	  right-hand side is the \(R\)-linear dual of the restricted module
2755	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M\) into the restricted ground ring. This is the dual
2756	  analogue of \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: it is to the internal hom
2757	  / dual exactly what that lemma is to the tensor product. It is the \emph{H2$'$
2758	  ingredient} of the restriction-compatibility isomorphism of the C-bridge
2759	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} --- the ring-iso/dual commutation that, composed with
2760	  the open-immersion pushforward comparison H1, identifies the dual of a restriction
2761	  with the restriction of the dual.
2762	\end{lemma}
2763	
2764	\begin{proof}
2765	  The two assignments \(\varphi \mapsto e^{-1} \circ \varphi\) and
2766	  \(\psi \mapsto e \circ \psi\) are mutually inverse, since \(e \circ e^{-1} =
2767	  \mathrm{id}_S\) and \(e^{-1} \circ e = \mathrm{id}_R\) as ring homomorphisms, so the
2768	  two composites are the identity on functionals. Each assignment is \(R\)-linear:
2769	  additivity is immediate, and for a scalar \(r \in R\) the defining relation
2770	  \(r \cdot m = e(r) \cdot m\) of the pulled-back module structure makes each linearity
2771	  check a single rewrite --- \(e^{-1}\) (resp.\ \(e\)) intertwines the \(R\)-action on
2772	  the source with the \(R\)-action on the target precisely because \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\)
2773	  are inverse ring homomorphisms identifying \(R\) with \(S\). The forward map is
2774	  therefore an \(R\)-linear bijection, i.e.\ an \(R\)-linear equivalence; packaging the
2775	  two one-sided maps with the inverse identities gives
2776	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv}\).
2777	\end{proof}
2778	
2779	\begin{definition}
2780	
2781	\leanok
2782	[Precomposition equivalence on dual sections]
2783	  \label{def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}
2784	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dualPrecompEquiv}
2785	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual}
2786	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings and let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\)
2787	  be an isomorphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. On a fixed open \(U\), precomposition
2788	  with \(e\) gives an \(R(U)\)-linear equivalence between the dual sections
2789	  \[
2790	    \mathtt{dualPrecompEquiv}\,e\,U \;\colon\;
2791	      \bigl(M'|_U \to R|_U\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \bigl(M|_U \to R|_U\bigr),
2792	      \qquad \varphi \;\longmapsto\; \varphi \circ e|_U,
2793	  \]
2794	  with inverse \(\psi \mapsto \psi \circ (e^{-1})|_U\). This is the section-level core of
2795	  the contravariant functoriality of the presheaf dual \(M \mapsto M^\vee\)
2796	  (\cref{def:presheaf_dual}) in isomorphisms: precomposing a functional on \(M'\) with
2797	  the comparison isomorphism produces a functional on \(M\), \(R(U)\)-linearly and
2798	  invertibly.
2799	\end{definition}
2800	
2801	\begin{definition}
2802	
2803	\leanok
2804	[The presheaf dual carries an iso \(M \cong M'\) to an iso \(M'^\vee \cong M^\vee\)]
2805	  \label{def:presheaf_dual_iso_of_iso}
2806	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dualIsoOfIso}
2807	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}
2808	  An isomorphism \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules induces a
2809	  canonical isomorphism of presheaf duals
2810	  \[
2811	    \mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e \;\colon\; M'^\vee \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M^\vee
2812	  \]
2813	  --- note the reversal of direction, the dual being contravariant --- assembled
2814	  open-by-open from the precomposition equivalences
2815	  \(\mathtt{dualPrecompEquiv}\,e\,U\) of \cref{def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}.
2816	  Naturality in \(U\), i.e.\ compatibility of these sectionwise equivalences with
2817	  restriction, is the routine check that precomposition by \(e\) commutes with
2818	  restricting functionals.
2819	\end{definition}

2821	\begin{definition}
2822	
2823	\leanok
2824	[The sheaf-level dual carries an iso \(M \cong M'\) to an iso \(\mathtt{dual}\,M' \cong \mathtt{dual}\,M\)]
2825	  \label{def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso}
2826	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dualIsoOfIso}
2827	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, def:presheaf_dual_iso_of_iso}

2830	  duals
2831	  \[
2832	    \mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e \;\colon\; \mathtt{dual}\,M' \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2833	      \mathtt{dual}\,M,
2834	  \]
2835	  obtained by sheafifying the presheaf isomorphism \(\mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e\) of
