2843	\begin{lemma}
2844	
2845	\leanok
2846	[Open-immersion slice-site sheaf equivalence (the shared slice bridge)]
2847	  \label{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
2848	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overSliceSheafEquiv}
2849	  % SOURCE: Mathlib code-location provenance (this is NOT an external literature
2850	  % citation, so it carries no verbatim mathematical SOURCE QUOTE). This lemma
2851	  % completes the documented TODO at Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean, lines
2852	  % 19--22, reproduced here for orientation:
2853	  %   "show that both functors of the equivalence overEquivalence U are continuous
2854	  %    and induce an equivalence between Sheaf ((Opens.grothendieckTopology X).over U) A
2855	  %    and Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology U) A for any category A."
2856	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subset, with associated open
2857	  immersion \(\iota : U \hookrightarrow X\). The reindexing equivalence
2858	  \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U : \mathtt{Over}\,U \simeq \mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\)
2859	  --- between the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\) and the opens of the subspace
2860	  \(\widetilde{U}\) --- upgrades to an \emph{equivalence of sheaf categories}
2861	  \[
2862	    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr)\,A
2863	      \;\;\simeq\;\;
2864	    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,U\bigr)\,A
2865	  \]
2866	  for any target category \(A\), compatible with the module-pullback comparison
2867	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). This completes the Mathlib TODO recorded at
2868	  \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22). It is the \emph{shared root}
2869	  underlying both the restriction-compatibility isomorphism for the dual
2870	  (\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}) and the morphism-descent / gluing engine
2871	  (\cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}): both reduce to identifying a
2872	  slice-indexed family of sections over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) with an
2873	  open-immersion-indexed family over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\), and that
2874	  identification is exactly this equivalence.
2875	\end{lemma}
2876	
2877	\begin{proof}
2878	  The functor \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U\) realises \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) as a
2879	  \emph{dense subsite} of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\): every open of the subspace
2880	  \(\widetilde{U}\) is covered by down-sets \(\{V : V \le U\}\), and these down-sets are
2881	  precisely the objects of the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\). One therefore applies the
2882	  dense-subsite transfer \(\mathtt{Functor.IsDenseSubsite.sheafEquiv}\): a continuous,
2883	  cocontinuous, dense inclusion of sites induces an equivalence of the associated sheaf
2884	  categories. The continuity and cover-lifting hypotheses are supplied by the slice-site
2885	  instances of \(\mathtt{Sites/Over}\) (the Grothendieck topology
2886	  \(J.\mathtt{over}\,U\) and its comparison with the topology pulled back along
2887	  \(\iota\)).
2888	
2889	  The over-category coherence that is laborious for a general site --- the pseudofunctor
2890	  identities \(\mathtt{Over.mapId}\), \(\mathtt{Over.mapComp}\) and the associativity
2891	  \(\mathtt{overMapPullback\_assoc}\) --- is here \emph{automatic}: because
2892	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) is a thin poset, every hom-set is a subsingleton, so each such
2893	  square commutes by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) (and its heterogeneous form
2894	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.helim}\) where source and target objects differ only up to a
2895	  propositional equality of opens). The sole sectionwise content is the down-set
2896	  identification
2897	  \[
2898	    \iota_{!}\bigl(\iota^{-1}(V)\bigr) \;=\; V
2899	    \qquad (V \le U),
2900	  \]
2901	  i.e.\ the image under the open immersion of the preimage of an open contained in \(U\)
2902	  is that open again. Transporting sections along this equality and invoking the
2903	  dense-subsite equivalence yields the stated sheaf equivalence, compatible with
2904	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) by the same thinness argument.
2905	\end{proof}
2906	
2907	\begin{lemma}
2908	[The dual of a line bundle is a line bundle]
2909	  \label{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
2910	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_isLocallyTrivial}
2911	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
2912	        lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
2913	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2914	  % modules), Lemma~lemma-constructions-invertible (item~2)
2915	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200--L4213).
2916	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2917	  % "\item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
2918	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
2919	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2920	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
2921	  % is an isomorphism."
2922	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR,
2923	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (item~2).}
2924	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with
2925	  \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\). Then the sheaf-level dual
2926	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} is again locally trivial of
2927	  rank one, i.e.\ \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,L)\).
2928	\end{lemma}
2929	
2930	\begin{proof}
2931	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
2932	        lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
2933	  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an open \(U\) with a
2934	  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The crux is the
2935	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism
2936	  \[
2937	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f \;\cong\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
2938	  \]
2939	  for an open immersion \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\). Its first three steps run exactly
2940	  as for the closed linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, because both
2941	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) and the tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) are of the form
2942	  \(\mathtt{sheafification}.\mathrm{obj}\) of a presheaf:
2943	  \begin{enumerate}
2944	    \item reduce restriction to the abstract pullback,
2945	      \((-)|_f \cong (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via
2946	      \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\);
2947	    \item move the pullback inside the sheafification,
2948	      \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \cong
2949	      \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\), via
2950	      \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\);
2951	    \item strip the outer sheafification with \(\mathtt{.mapIso}\), reducing the claim
2952	      to the presheaf-level comparison.
2953	  \end{enumerate}
2954	  These three steps, together with the open-immersion pushforward comparison \emph{H1},
2955	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) (the identification of
2956	  two left adjoints of the same pushforward, \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\)
2957	  together with \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\)), are reused \emph{verbatim} from
2958	  the tensor and do typecheck for the dual.
2959	
2960	  Here the parallel with the tensor \emph{ends}. After H1 the residual to close is
2961	  \[
2962	    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,A)
2963	      \;\cong\;
2964	    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,A\bigr),
2965	  \]
2966	  i.e.\ the open-immersion pushforward commutes with the presheaf dual. For the tensor
2967	  the corresponding residual closed through the ring-iso commutation, because
2968	  \(M \otimes_X N\) is \emph{sectionwise} \(M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)\) --- a literal
2969	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\)-image, to which the strong-monoidal comparison applies.
2970	  The dual is \emph{not} of this form: \(\mathtt{dual} = \mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
2971	  is the \emph{slice} internal hom, whose value at an open \(V\) is a module of
2972	  morphisms over the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\), not a sectionwise
2973	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\)-image. Consequently the ring-iso/dual commutation
2974	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv} is only the \emph{ModuleCat-level shadow}
2975	  of the comparison; it does \emph{not} by itself discharge the residual, and the naive
2976	  ``verbatim mirror'' \emph{H2$'$} of the tensor's ring-iso step does not close it.
2977	
2978	  The residual is the classical presheaf-level comparison: for an open immersion
2979	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) the open-immersion pushforward commutes with the presheaf dual,
2980	  \[
2981	    f_*\mathcal{H}\!om(A, \mathcal{O}) \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}\!om(f_*A, f_*\mathcal{O}).
2982	  \]
2983	  This comparison is constructed objectwise. For each open \(V \subseteq U\), both sides
2984	  compute an \(\mathcal{O}(V)\)-module of \(\mathcal{O}|_V\)-linear maps; the only
2985	  difference is that the left-hand side carries the \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-module structure
2986	  and the right-hand side the \(\mathcal{O}_U(V)\)-module structure, which the sectionwise
2987	  ring isomorphism \(f^\#_V \colon \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_U(V)\)
2988	  identifies canonically. The per-section bridge is exactly
2989	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}; assembling into a presheaf natural
2990	  isomorphism uses only that \(\mathrm{Opens}\) is a thin category, so all naturality
2991	  squares commute automatically. Combining this comparison with the trivialisation gives
2992	  \[
2993	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U
2994	      \;\cong\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_U\bigr)
2995	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
2996	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U,
2997	  \]
2998	  the last identification being evaluation at \(1\). Hence \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is
2999	  locally trivial of rank one, as required by item~2 of Stacks
3000	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (tag 01CR).
3001	
3002	  \emph{Route note (iter-230, settled empirically).} Do not attempt to discharge the
3003	  residual via the fixed-value-category sheaf-site equivalence
3004	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}: that equivalence is parametric in a
3005	  fixed value category and cannot transport the varying-ring \(\mathcal{O}(V)\)-module
3006	  fibration the presheaf dual carries. The objectwise route above is correct and avoids
3007	  that detour.
3008	\end{proof}
3009	
3010	\begin{lemma}
3011	
3012	\leanok
3013	[Local isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules glue to a global isomorphism]
3014	  \label{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}
3015	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_restrict}
3016	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi \colon M \to N\) a morphism in
3017	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\).
3018	  Suppose that for every point \(x \in X\) there is an open neighbourhood \(U_x\) of
3019	  \(x\) such that the restriction of \(\varphi\) to \(U_x\) --- the image
3020	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) under the
3021	  restriction functor along the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\colon U_x \to X\) ---
3022	  is an isomorphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_x\). Then \(\varphi\) is an
3023	  isomorphism.
3024	\end{lemma}
3025	
3026	\begin{proof}
3027	  Being an isomorphism for a morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules can be
3028	  checked on the underlying morphism of sheaves of abelian groups, and there it can be
3029	  checked stalkwise. Fix a point \(x \in X\). Choose a preimage \(x' \in U_x\) of \(x\)
3030	  under the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\). By hypothesis the restriction
3031	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) is an
3032	  isomorphism, hence so is its image under any functor --- in particular under the
3033	  stalk-at-\(x'\) functor on the underlying ab-sheaves. The natural isomorphism
3034	  ``restriction commutes with stalks'' (\(\mathtt{restrictStalkNatIso}\)) identifies
3035	  this with the stalk of (the underlying ab-sheaf morphism of) \(\varphi\) at the image
3036	  point \(x\); transporting along it shows that stalk is an isomorphism. As this holds
3037	  at every \(x\), and a morphism of sheaves of abelian groups all of whose stalks are
3038	  isomorphisms is itself an isomorphism (stalkwise iso-detection,
3039	  \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_stalkFunctor\_map\_iso}\) on \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\)), the
3040	  underlying ab-sheaf morphism of \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism. Finally the forgetful
3041	  functor \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules.toPresheaf}\) reflects isomorphisms, so \(\varphi\)
3042	  itself is an isomorphism.
3043	\end{proof}
3044	
3045	\begin{definition}
3046	
3047	
3048	\leanok
3049	[Scheme-level linearity wrapper \(\mathtt{homMk}\) (A-bridge step (ii))]
3050	  \label{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
3051	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homMk}
3052	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Given a morphism
3053	  \(g \colon M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf} \to N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}\) of
3054	  the underlying presheaves of abelian groups, together with a hypothesis \(hg\) that
3055	  \(g\) is \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear sectionwise, \(\mathtt{homMk}\,g\,hg\) is the
3056	  morphism \(M \to N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) that this data determines. It
3057	  is a thin wrapper that packages \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.homMk}\) at the scheme
3058	  level: the sectionwise-linearity datum promotes the ab-group morphism to a genuine
3059	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Its \(\mathtt{@[simp]}\) companion
3060	  \(\mathtt{toPresheaf\_map\_homMk}\) records that forgetting back recovers \(g\), i.e.\
3061	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,X).\mathtt{map}\,(\mathtt{homMk}\,g\,hg) = g\). This is step
3062	  (ii) of the A-bridge \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}: once the
3063	  underlying ab-sheaf morphism has been glued, \(\mathtt{homMk}\) re-attaches the
3064	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linearity to produce the global morphism of modules.
3065	\end{definition}
3066	
3067	\begin{lemma}
3068	[Compatible local morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules glue to a global morphism]
3069	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}
3070	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homOfLocalCompat}
3071	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and \(\{U_i\}\) an
3072	  open cover of \(X\). Suppose given, for each \(i\), a morphism
3073	  \(f_i \colon M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_i\) such that
3074	  for all \(i, j\) the restrictions of \(f_i\) and \(f_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree.
3075	  Then there is a unique global morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
3076	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) whose restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\).
3077	\end{lemma}
3078	
3079	\begin{proof}
3080	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_homMk, lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
3081	  A morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is, concretely, a morphism of the
3082	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups that is in addition \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear
3083	  sectionwise. We build \(f\) in two steps.
3084	
3085	  \emph{(i) Glue the underlying ab-sheaf morphism.} Forget \(M\) and \(N\) to their
3086	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups. The morphisms-between-them presheaf
3087	  \(\mathcal{H} := \mathtt{presheafHom}\,(M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\,
3088	  (N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\), whose value at an open \(W\) is the abelian
3089	  group of morphisms \(M|_W \to N|_W\) of presheaves of abelian groups, is itself a
3090	  \emph{sheaf of types} --- this is the ``hom into a sheaf is a sheaf'' principle
3091	  \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsSheaf.hom}\), which consumes the sheaf condition of \(N\).
3092	  Regarding \(\mathcal{H}\) as a \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\), its universal gluing
3093	  property \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) amalgamates any compatible family of local
3094	  sections of \(\mathcal{H}\) into a unique global section. This route is preferred over
3095	  the raw sieve-level amalgamation \(\mathtt{presheafHom\_isSheafFor}\), which carries
3096	  strictly more bookkeeping.
3097	
3098	  The load-bearing datum is, for each \(i\), the \emph{local section}
3099	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i \in \mathcal{H}.\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,U_i)\)
3100	  manufactured from \(f_i\). Its component at a pair \((V, h : V \to U_i)\) is the given
3101	  component of \(f_i\), conjugated by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications
3102	  that arise from the open-set equality \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\) valid
3103	  for \(V \subseteq U_i\) (the image under the open immersion \(\iota_i : U_i \to X\) of
3104	  the preimage of \(V\) is \(V\) again). The genuine coherence risk of the whole
3105	  construction is concentrated in the \emph{naturality field} of
3106	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\): the requirement that these
3107	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugated components of \(f_i\) commute across the morphisms of
3108	  the slice category \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\). This is the one sub-piece that should be
3109	  built and verified first, as a standalone axiom-clean lemma.
3110	
3111	  This \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality is precisely the \emph{section-direction
3112	  slice} of the comparison packaged in \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}:
3113	  converting the local datum \(f_i\), which lives over the open-immersion pullback
3114	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U_i}\), into a section of \(\mathcal{H}\) indexed over the
3115	  slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\) is the one-sided realisation of the open-immersion
3116	  \(\leftrightarrow\) slice transport. As there, the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation is
3117	  controlled by the down-set identity \(\iota_i(\iota_i^{-1}(V)) = V\) for
3118	  \(V \subseteq U_i\), and the coherence is automatic on the thin poset
3119	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). The dual restriction-iso
3120	  (\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}) consumes the \emph{functor-direction} face of the
3121	  same equivalence; that the two share \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} as a
3122	  common root is why the shared bridge, once built, unblocks both at once.
3123	
3124	  The remaining four sub-steps are mechanical: (a) the
3125	  cocycle/\(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) condition on the family
3126	  \((\mathtt{localSection}\,i)_i\), which is exactly the assumed agreement of \(f_i\)
3127	  and \(f_j\) on the overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\); (b) gluing via
3128	  \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) and converting the amalgamated section over
3129	  \(\mathrm{op}\,\top\) into a genuine morphism \(g \colon M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}
3130	  \to N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}\) of the underlying presheaves --- the
3131	  \(\top\)-section-to-morphism passage being the global-sections equivalence
3132	  \(\mathtt{presheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) / \(\mathtt{sheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) of the
3133	  hom-sheaf \(\mathcal{H}\) (which, since \(\top\) is terminal in \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\),
3134	  lands the glued section as an honest morphism of presheaves), \emph{not} a hand-unfold
3135	  of the \(\top\)-section; (c) the
3136	  sectionwise linearity check; and (d) the recovery that the restriction of \(g\) to
3137	  each \(U_i\) is the ab-sheaf morphism underlying \(f_i\).
3138	
3139	  \emph{(ii) Promote to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear.} The linearity equation
3140	  \(g(r \cdot m) = r \cdot g(m)\) is a sectionwise identity between two sections of a
3141	  separated presheaf. It holds on each \(U_i\), because there \(g\) restricts to the
3142	  module morphism \(f_i\); since the two sides agree on the cover \(\{U_i\}\) and the
3143	  ambient presheaf is separated, they agree globally. Packaging \(g\) together with this
3144	  global linearity through the scheme-level wrapper \cref{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
3145	  (\(\mathtt{homMk}\)) yields a morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
3146	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); its restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\) and its
3147	  uniqueness are both inherited from the ab-sheaf amalgamation of step (i).
3148	
3149	  This gluing engine is substantially larger than a routine glue: it is dominated by the
3150	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality field and the convert-and-recover bookkeeping, and
3151	  the naturality is the section-direction slice of
3152	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} --- so its true cost is bounded below by
3153	  building that shared bridge, not by the surrounding assembly. It is therefore to be
3154	  scoped as a multi-piece build --- with \(\mathtt{localSection}\), together with its
3155	  naturality, built as a standalone axiom-clean lemma first --- rather than a one-shot.
3156	  The step computes no tensor stalk --- it is purely a statement about gluing morphisms
3157	  of sheaves --- and so it does not re-enter the abandoned tensor-stalk commutation.
3158	\end{proof}

3160	\begin{remark}
3161	[How the dual infrastructure discharges the \(\otimes\)-inverse]
3162	  \label{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}
3163	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial,
3164	        lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat, lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict,
3165	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
3166	  Once \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,def:presheaf_dual,%
3167	  lem:internal_hom_isSheaf,lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} are available,
3168	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is discharged with no further missing object:
3169	  take \(L^{-1} := \mathtt{dual}\,L\) (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}), which is a line
3170	  bundle by \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}. The global contraction \(\varepsilon_L
3171	  \colon L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{not} obtained by sheafifying
3172	  the presheaf-level evaluation; it is instead built by \emph{gluing the canonical local
3173	  contraction morphisms}. On each trivialising open \(U\), the
3174	  restriction-compatibility iso \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} carries
3175	  \((L \otimes_X L^{-1})|_U\) to \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U\), and the
3176	  canonical local contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\)
3177	  of the free rank-one module with its dual is the left unitor
3178	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, an isomorphism. These local contractions are canonical,
3179	  hence agree on overlaps, and so glue --- by the A-bridge
3180	  \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat} --- to a single global morphism
3181	  \(\varepsilon_L \colon L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\). Since ``being an
3182	  isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) and \(\varepsilon_L\) restricts to an isomorphism on
3183	  each \(U\), it is a global isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim}
3184	  \mathcal{O}_X\) by the B-connector \cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}.
3185

3187	  ``sheafify-the-evaluation'' route --- which would re-enter the abandoned
3188	  tensor-stalk / sheafification-unit whiskering commutation --- and computes no tensor
3189	  stalk anywhere. It is the realization the ``infrastructure-blocked'' note in
3190	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} points to.

3193	\begin{remark}
3194	[Alternative route via object-level descent (fallback)]
3195	  \label{rem:dual_via_stack}
3196	  Should the slice-and-sheafify route of
3197	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} bottom out, the dual can
3198	  instead be assembled by \emph{object-level descent}: glue the local trivial duals

3200	  \(g_{ij}^{-\mathsf{T}}\) of \(L\) into a global \(L^{-1}\) on \(X\). Abstractly this
3201	  is effective descent for sheaves of modules on the small Zariski site --- the
3202	  essential-surjectivity of the descent-data functor of an \(\mathtt{IsStack}\)

1	\chapter{The Jacobian as an abelian variety}
2	\label{chap:Jacobian}
3	Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(k\) in the sense of Chapter~\ref{chap:Genus}, with genus \(g = g(C)\).
4	The Jacobian is built as the Albanese object of \(C\) in the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), and the construction stratifies by genus. For positive genus the witness \emph{object} is the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) produced by \textbf{Route~A (the Picard scheme via FGA representability)}: this is the project's committed critical path, and it is \emph{mandatory} for the positive-genus object regardless of any other route choice --- the object must be a genuine \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety even when \(C(k) = \emptyset\) (see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\) and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites are the single largest Mathlib build-out the project gates on. For genus \(0\) the witness object is instead the trivial scheme \(\Spec k\), \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A's representability engine; the only remaining content in that arm is a rigidity \emph{statement}, the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} developed in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} via the Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (Rigidity Lemma \(+\) additivity Corollary~1.5 \(+\) Corollary~1.2 \(+\) rational-map extension), \emph{cube-free} and \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality argument. The over-\(\bar k\) declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, which still carries \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\), is the retained fallback-(a) artifact and is \emph{not} the declaration the genus-\(0\) witness consumes.
5	\section{The Albanese construction}
6	For a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\), the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from \(C\). Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
7	\subsection{Symmetric powers and the Abel--Jacobi map}
8	Let \(C^{(g)} = \Sym^g(C)\) be the \(g\)\textsuperscript{th} symmetric power of \(C\) (the quotient of \(C^g\) by the action of the symmetric group \(S_g\)). It is a smooth proper scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g\). Fix a \(k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(k)\). The map
9	\[
10	  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
11	  \sum_{i=1}^g [P_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g P_i - g P_0\bigr)
12	\]
13	is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing \(P_0\) gives a translation isomorphism \(\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}\), and the Stein factorisation of \(\sigma\) yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.
14	In the Lean formalisation we do not have general symmetric powers of schemes (or quotients by finite group actions) in Mathlib. We therefore take a more direct approach: we define the Jacobian as the Albanese object of \(C\) inside the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), using the universal property and the known dimension formula.
15	\subsection{The Albanese universal property}
16	\begin{definition}\leanok
17	[Albanese universal property of a pointed curve]
18	  \label{def:IsAlbanese}
19	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}
20	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Remark rmk:Alb (the Albanese map), FGA Explained ch.~5; arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
21	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Reversing the preceding argument, we see that every
22	  % such \(b\) arises from a unique map \(a\:B^*\to \IPic_{X/k}\) such that
23	  % \(a(0)=0\).  Since \(B^*\) is integral, \(a\) factors through \(P\).  Thus the
24	  % maps \(a\:B^*\to P\) and \(b\:X\to B\) are in bijective correspondence.
25	  % Plainly, this correspondence is compatible with maps \(b'\:B\to B'\) such
26	  % that \(b'(0)=0\).  In particular, \(1_P\) corresponds to a natural map
27	  % \(u\:X\to A\) such that \(u(x)=0\), and every map \(b\:X\to B\) factors
28	  % uniquely through \(u\)."
29	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Remark (the Albanese map, rmk:Alb).}
30	  Let \((C, P)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) with a marked \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\). A smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) is an \emph{Albanese object} for \((C, P)\) if there exists a morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) sending \(P\) to the identity element \(\eta \in J\), and such that every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\) factors uniquely as \(f = \iota \circ g\) for some morphism of group schemes \(g \colon J \to A\).
31	\end{definition}
32	\begin{remark}
33	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_typeclasses}
34	  The Lean encoding of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese} takes the four abelian-variety conditions on \(J\) --- group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility --- as \emph{typeclass parameters} of the declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}, not as conjuncts inside the body. The body is the existential statement on \(\iota\) and its universal-factorisation property; the four hypotheses on \(J\) sit on the binder of the definition. A downstream user constructs an \texttt{IsAlbanese} term against these instances and threads them at every use site (cf.\ the \texttt{letI} chain in \texttt{AbelJacobi.ofCurve} which installs all four instances on \(\Jac(C)\) before applying any Albanese consequence). The same convention is used for the target abelian variety \(A\) in the universal-property clause.
35	\end{remark}
36	\subsection{Extracting the universal morphism}
37	An \texttt{IsAlbanese} term \(h\) packages, behind a single existential, the universal pointed morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) together with its two characterising properties: the pointed condition \(P \circ \iota = \eta_J\) and the universal-factorisation property of \(\iota\). The data and the two properties are extracted from the existential body by the following trio of helpers, each implemented in Lean via \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} on the existential content of \texttt{IsAlbanese}.
38	\begin{definition}
39	\leanok
40	[The Albanese universal morphism]
41	  \label{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
42	  
43	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.ofCurve}
44	  Given a pointed curve \((C, P)\) and an Albanese term \(h \colon \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J\), the \emph{universal morphism} of \(h\) is the morphism
45	  \[
46	    \iota := h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J,
47	  \]
48	  obtained by \texttt{Classical.choose} on the existential body of \(h\).
49	\end{definition}
50	\begin{lemma}
51	\leanok
52	[Pointed condition for the universal morphism]
53	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}
54	  
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.comp_ofCurve}
56	  The universal morphism \(h.\mathtt{ofCurve}\) sends the marked point \(P\) to the identity of \(J\):
57	  \[
58	    P \circ h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \;=\; \eta_J.
59	  \]
60	\end{lemma}
61	\begin{lemma}
62	\leanok
63	[Universal factorisation through the Albanese object]
64	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
65	  
66	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
67	  For every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) over \(k\) with \(P \circ f = \eta_A\), there exists a unique morphism \(g \colon J \to A\) such that \(f = h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \circ g\).
68	\end{lemma}
69	This extraction API is precisely what the protected interface \(\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}\) of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} consumes: each protected wrapper takes a marked point \(P \in C(k)\) as input, projects the per-point Albanese data from the bundled witness \texttt{JacobianWitness} (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) at that \(P\), and applies the corresponding helper above.
70	\begin{theorem}\leanok
71	[Uniqueness of the Albanese object]
72	  \label{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
73	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.unique}
74	  Let \(J_1, J_2\) be two Albanese objects for the same pointed curve \((C, P)\), with universal pointed morphisms \(\iota_1 := h_1.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_1\) and \(\iota_2 := h_2.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_2\) (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}). Then there is a unique morphism \(e \colon J_1 \to J_2\) satisfying \(\iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e\). The proof is the standard universal-property argument: each of the two universal morphisms factors through the other, the two composites equal the unique self-factorisation \(\mathrm{id}\), and the produced morphism \(e\) is therefore an isomorphism; only the existence and uniqueness of \(e\) as a morphism are exposed in the conclusion (see Remark~\ref{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}).
75	\end{theorem}
76	\begin{remark}
77	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}
78	  The Lean proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique} internally produces an inverse morphism \(h \colon J_2 \to J_1\) and verifies \(g \circ h = \mathrm{id}_{J_1}\) and \(h \circ g = \mathrm{id}_{J_2}\) (the invertibility witnesses are computed as intermediate lemmas of the tactic block) before returning the triple \((g,\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ g,\ \text{uniqueness})\). The invertibility witnesses are computed but not retained in the return type
79	  \[
80	    \exists! (e : J_1 \to J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.
81	  \]
82	  The natural strengthening would be to return
83	  \[
84	    \exists! (e : J_1 \cong J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.\mathtt{hom},
85	  \]
86	  packaging the produced morphism together with its inverse. Tightening the conclusion in this way is a Lean-side refactor of the signature of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} that the project may carry out in a future iteration. The downstream consumers of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} (the protected \texttt{AbelJacobi.Jacobian.*} interface) use only the morphism-and-uniqueness content of the conclusion, so the current weaker conclusion is sufficient for the present API.
87	\end{remark}
88	\subsection{The Jacobian definition}
89	\begin{definition}\leanok
90	[Jacobian]
91	  \uses{def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
92	  \label{def:Jacobian}
93	  
94	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian}
95	  The \emph{Jacobian} of \(C\) is the abelian variety \(\Jac(C)\) over \(k\) obtained as the Albanese object of \(C\): a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g(C)\) equipped with a universal morphism \(C \to \Jac(C)\) (for each choice of marked point). It is viewed as an object of the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\). Formally, \(\Jac(C)\) is defined as the underlying scheme of a (uniform-over-\(P\)) Albanese witness for \(C\) provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}.
96	  When \(g(C) = 0\) the Jacobian collapses to \(\Spec k\) itself, which is the terminal object in the category of \(k\)-schemes. When \(g(C) > 0\) the underlying scheme is the abelian variety produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}; the genus-one case recovers \(C\) itself (after choosing a \(k\)-rational point), and the higher genus case yields the non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety. Both cases are handled uniformly: \(\Jac(C)\) is the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness, with no special-case branching at the level of the definition. The genus-\(0\) specialisation is implicit in the fact that any group scheme over \(k\) of relative dimension \(0\) is isomorphic to \(\Spec k\).
97	\end{definition}
98	\section{Group scheme structure and abelian-variety properties}
99	The four protected instances on \(\Jac(C)\) are each obtained, at the level of the Lean formalisation, by projecting a field of the underlying Albanese witness produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}. The mathematical content of each property is the corresponding statement about the Albanese variety of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve.
100	\begin{theorem}\leanok
101	[Group object structure]
102	  \label{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}
103	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGrpObj}
104	  \(\Jac(C)\) carries a canonical group-object structure in the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\).
105	\end{theorem}
106	\begin{proof}
107	  By Definition~\ref{def:Jacobian}, \(\Jac(C) = (\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C).J\) as a \(k\)-scheme, where \(\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C\) is the Albanese witness extracted from Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} via \texttt{Classical.choice}. The witness carries the group-object structure as its field \(\mathtt{grpObj} : \mathrm{GrpObj}\, J\), so \(\Jac(C)\) inherits it by projection.
108	  Mathematically, any Albanese variety carries a unique group-object structure compatible with its universal pointed morphism. Indeed, if \(\iota \colon C \to J\) is the universal morphism sending the marked point \(P\) to the identity, then the diagonal \(C \times C \to J \times J\), post-composed with the universal morphism out of \(C \times C\) (factored through \(J\) by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}), produces the multiplication; the unique morphism to a one-point pointed scheme produces the inverse; the marked point produces the identity. Uniqueness of the resulting group-object structure is again the universal-property argument of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}.
109	\end{proof}
110	\begin{theorem}\leanok
111	[Smoothness and dimension]
112	  \label{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus}

133	\begin{proof}
134	  The Lean formalisation projects the field \(\mathtt{smoothGenus} : \mathrm{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,(\genus\, C)\,J.\mathtt{hom}\) of the witness \(\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C\); the relative dimension \(g(C)\) is recorded directly in the witness so that no separate dimension calculation is needed on the Lean side.
135	  Mathematically, the dimension of an Albanese variety of \(C\) equals the genus \(g = g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) (Phase~A, Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafAb}). The tangent space to \(\Jac(C)\) at the identity is canonically identified with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) by deformation theory of the Picard functor: an infinitesimal deformation of \(\mathcal O_C\) is classified by an element of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\), and this identifies the tangent space at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\). Smoothness of \(\Jac(C)\) over \(k\) follows from the smoothness of any group scheme over a field whose tangent space at the identity is of constant rank equal to the relative dimension --- a consequence of homogeneity of the group action, since translation by any \(k\)-rational point is an automorphism and so the local structure of \(\Jac(C)\) at every \(k\)-rational point is the same as at the identity.
136	\end{proof}

180	  Mathematically, the Albanese variety \(\Jac(C)\) is the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (the Picard scheme), and the identity component of a group scheme of finite type over a field is geometrically irreducible (in fact, geometrically connected, and since it is smooth at the identity by Theorem~\ref{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus} it is geometrically irreducible). Equivalently, in the symmetric-power route, \(\Jac(C)\) arises as the Stein factorisation of \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) followed by a translation; \(\Sym^g(C)\) is geometrically irreducible (being the quotient of \(C^g\), itself geometrically irreducible as a product of geometrically irreducible factors, by a finite group action), so its image under the Stein factorisation is geometrically irreducible as well.

182	\subsection{Sanity check in low genus}
183	\begin{itemize}
184	  \item \(g = 0\): Then \(\Jac(C) = \Spec k\), the trivial group scheme; \(\Jac(\mathbb P^1_k) = \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(0 = g\).
185	  \item \(g = 1\): Then \(\Jac(C) \cong C\) once a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\) is fixed; \(C\) is itself an elliptic curve (abelian variety of dimension \(1\)).
186	  \item \(g \ge 2\): \(\Jac(C)\) is a non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety, never isomorphic to \(C\) as a scheme.
187	\end{itemize}
188	\section{Existence of an Albanese variety}
189	The classical construction of the Jacobian goes through the Picard scheme machinery: \(\Jac(C)\) is the connected component of the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). An equivalent route, available whenever a \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\) has been fixed, is to take the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) and translate by \(-gP\).
190	\subsection{The Albanese witness bundle}
191	Before stating the existence theorem, we package its conclusion into a single structure --- the \emph{Albanese witness} of \(C\). This bundling reverses the quantifier order of the classical statement (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}) and is the data structure consumed by the \texttt{Jacobian} family of declarations in this file and by the \texttt{AbelJacobi} interface of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi}.
192	\begin{definition}
193	\leanok
194	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus}
195	[Albanese witness for a curve]
196	  \label{def:JacobianWitness}
197	  
198	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.JacobianWitness}
199	  An \emph{Albanese witness} for the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) over \(k\) is a bundle of the following seven fields:
200	  \begin{itemize}
201	    \item \(J\): an object of the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\) --- the candidate Albanese object.
202	    \item \(\mathtt{grpObj}\): a group-object structure on \(J\) in the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\).
203	    \item \(\mathtt{proper}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is proper.
204	    \item \(\mathtt{smooth}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is smooth.
205	    \item \(\mathtt{geomIrred}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is geometrically irreducible.
206	    \item \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\). This refines \(\mathtt{smooth}\) at the specific relative dimension \(\genus\, C\) (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}).
207	    \item \(\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\): a proof that, for every \(k\)-rational marked point \(P \in C(k)\), the scheme \(J\) together with its group-object, proper, smooth, and geometrically-irreducible structures satisfies the Albanese universal property for the pointed curve \((C, P)\) in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}.
208	  \end{itemize}
209	  Formally, an Albanese witness is a Lean-level record (a structure) whose name is \texttt{JacobianWitness}.
210	\end{definition}
211	\begin{remark}
212	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}
213	  The classical phrasing of the existence statement of an Albanese variety is
214	  \[
215	    \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \exists\, J,\ J \text{ is an Albanese object for } (C, P).
216	  \]
217	  The witness bundle reverses these quantifiers: a single \(J\) is chosen once (intrinsic to \(C\)), and the marked-point-dependent data is recorded as the family-valued field
218	  \[
219	    \mathtt{isAlbaneseFor} \colon \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J.
220	  \]
221	  Mathematically this reversal is justified because the underlying scheme of any Albanese object is intrinsic to \(C\): a different choice of \(P \in C(k)\) alters the universal morphism \(\iota_P \colon C \to J\) by a translation of \(J\), which is an automorphism of \(J\), and so leaves \(J\) unchanged as a scheme. The reversed bundling is what makes the per-\(P\) projection in Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} clean: \(\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}\), and \(\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}\) each take a marked point \(P\) as input and project the corresponding component of \(\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\,P\) via the extraction trio (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}, Lemmas~\ref{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}, \ref{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}).
222	\end{remark}
223	\begin{remark}
224	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}
225	  The fields \(\mathtt{smooth}\) and \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\) of an Albanese witness are mathematically redundant: the relative-dimension version implies the unqualified version via Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.SmoothOfRelativeDimension.smooth}. Keeping both fields is a Lean-level convenience: \(\mathtt{smooth}\) is the form required by the typeclass binder of \texttt{IsAlbanese} (and by the protected \(\mathtt{Jacobian.instGrpObj}\) projection), whereas \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\) is the form required by the protected \(\mathtt{Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}\) projection. The redundancy carries no mathematical cost (the two fields agree) and no soundness cost (deriving one from the other would only re-run the same Mathlib instance synthesis at the projection sites).
226	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus, def:JacobianWitness}
227	\end{remark}

247	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over a field \(k\). There exists a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) of relative dimension \(g(C)\) such that, for every \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\), the scheme \(J\) is an Albanese object for the pointed curve \((C, P)\) in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}. Equivalently, the type of Albanese witnesses for \(C\) (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) is non-empty.
248	\end{theorem}
249	\begin{proof}
250	  The proof is split into three classical constructions plus the genus-\(0\) sub-case absorbed into the same witness (the latter packaged as the explicit construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}, which routes through the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} --- rigidity proved over the algebraic closure \(\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k\) --- and then \emph{descends} the resulting morphism equality from \(\bar k\) to the base field \(k\) along the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\)). Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme \(J\); only the universal morphism \(\iota_P \colon C \to J\) varies with the marked point \(P\). We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
251	  \paragraph{(0) Reduction to a \(P\)-independent witness.}
252	  The witness \(J\) depends only on \(C\), not on the marked point \(P\). Indeed, both classical constructions below produce \(J\) as an intrinsic object of \(C\) (the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\), or the Stein-factorisation target of \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) translated by a fixed \(P_0\)); a different choice of \(P\) in \(C(k)\) alters \(\iota_P\) by a translation of \(J\), which is an automorphism, and so does not change \(J\) as a scheme. The Lean encoding reflects this by bundling \(J\) as a single field and the universal-property data as \(\forall P,\, \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\, C\, P\, J\). We now turn to producing \(J\).
253	  \paragraph{Route A --- Picard scheme.}
254	  This route follows Hartshorne~III.4 and FGA Explained, Chapter~9.
255	  \begin{itemize}
256	    \item[\textbf{(A.1)}] \emph{The relative Picard functor.} For a \(k\)-scheme \(T\), write \(C_T := C \times_k T\) and let \(\pi \colon C_T \to T\) be the projection. The relative Picard functor is
257	    \[
258	      \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k} \colon (\Sch/k)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \Sets, \qquad
259	      T \longmapsto \Pic(C_T) / \pi^*\Pic(T).
260	    \]
261	    A \(T\)-point is a line bundle on \(C_T\) modulo a line bundle pulled back from \(T\). The set-valued formulation suffices for representability; the canonical abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C_T)\) descends to the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) and gives \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) a natural group-functor refinement.
262	    \item[\textbf{(A.2)}] \emph{Representability.} For \(C\) smooth proper geometrically connected over \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point, \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) (after \'etale sheafification, if \(k\) is not algebraically closed) is representable by a \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), locally of finite type. This is FGA's main representability theorem (Grothendieck, FGA~232); the smooth-proper-geometrically-connected hypothesis ensures that the cohomology-and-base-change machinery applies and that \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = k\), which is needed for the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) to be \'etale-locally injective.
263	    \item[\textbf{(A.3)}] \emph{The identity component.} Let \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) be the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\). As the identity component of a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is geometrically irreducible; for \(C\) smooth proper of genus \(g\), it is moreover a smooth proper \(k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\). Smoothness comes from the deformation-theoretic identification of its tangent space at the identity with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) and homogeneity of the group action; properness from the fact that the natural map \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\) is an open and closed immersion onto a proper component (a consequence of the cohomological criterion: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the kernel of the degree map \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k\), which is locally constant). Take \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
264	    \item[\textbf{(A.4)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi universal morphism.} For each \(P \in C(k)\), the Abel--Jacobi morphism
265	    \[
266	      \iota_P \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}, \qquad
267	      Q \longmapsto [\mathcal O_C(Q - P)]
268	    \]
269	    sends \(P\) to the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). It is universal among morphisms \(f \colon C \to A\) to smooth proper geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group schemes \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\): this is the classical Albanese property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), proved by reducing to the case \(k = \overline k\) (Galois descent), where it is a standard consequence of the autoduality of the Jacobian and the fact that line bundles on \(C \times A\) trivialised along \(\{P\} \times A\) correspond bijectively to morphisms \(C \to A^{\vee} = A\) sending \(P\) to \(\eta_A\).
270	  \end{itemize}
271	  \emph{Mathlib status for Route A.} Each sub-step is missing.
272	  \begin{itemize}
273	    \item Sub-step A.1 requires a working theory of relative line bundles on a product \(C \times_k T\) and the descent of the abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\) along \(\pi^*\). The pieces are present individually but not assembled. (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 700\)--\(1100\) LOC, \(\sim 6\)--\(10\) iters.)
274	    \item Sub-step A.2 requires FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\), which in turn requires Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure not in Mathlib (\texttt{HilbertScheme}, \texttt{QuotScheme}, and the cohomology-and-base-change for proper morphisms with finitely presented kernels). The construction engine for this Quot/Hilbert layer is Nitsure, ``Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes'' (FGA Explained; arXiv:math/0504590), \S\,4 (generic flatness and the flattening stratification) and \S\,5 (the construction of \(\mathrm{Quot}\), hence \(\mathrm{Hilb}\), as a scheme); see \texttt{references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.md}. (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 2200\)--\(3000\) LOC, \(\sim 15\)--\(25\) iters --- the dominant Route A block.)
275	    \item Sub-step A.3 requires connectedness machinery for group schemes locally of finite type, the identity-component construction \(G^0 \subseteq G\), and the degree map \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k\). (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 600\)--\(900\) LOC, \(\sim 5\)--\(8\) iters.)
276	    \item Sub-step A.4 requires the universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), which is the classical Albanese functoriality and is not in Mathlib in this form. \textbf{Bypass via Milne autoduality FAILS}: as Milne III \S~6 writes the proof of Proposition~6.1, it invokes Theorem~3.2 (rational-map-extension from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety) directly to upgrade the symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular map \(J \to A\), and the would-be Picard-functor / autoduality detour \(J \cong J^\vee\) routes through the theorem of the cube, excised from this project iter-163. Sub-step~A.4 therefore inherits the Theorem~3.2 + Lemma~3.3 codim-\(1\) extension + Weil-divisor API + Auslander--Buchsbaum sub-build (the last absent from Mathlib). (See iter-172 audit at \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP}, NOTE block + \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} proof.) (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 2500{+}\) LOC, \(\sim 22\)--\(35\) iters.)
277	  \end{itemize}

333	      with no Mathlib Weil-divisor support; see
334	      \cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}) is the dominant LOC driver.
335	      Additionally, A.4 inherits a symmetric-powers-of-schemes prerequisite
336	      shared with the historical Route~B. (See iter-172 audit at

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of \(\Jac(C)\). It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let \(k\) be a field. Let \(X\) be a reduced geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme and let \(Y\) be a separated \(k\)-scheme. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y\) be two morphisms of \(k\)-schemes, and let \(U \subseteq X\) be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where \(U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X\) is the canonical open immersion), then \(g_1 = g_2\) as morphisms of \(k\)-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where \(Y\) is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism \(\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}\) formed from the group law on \(Y\). The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side \([\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) to \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\).
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{\(Y \to \Spec k\) is separated.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) provides this directly (no projection from \(\mathtt{IsProper}\) needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)\) that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{\(X\) is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]\) over the field-spectrum base \(\Spec k\) (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields \(\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}\) via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion \(U.\iota\) is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]\), the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality \(g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}\) from the agreement-on-\(U\) hypothesis. The promotion to \(g_1 = g_2\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\) uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of \(X.\mathrm{left}\).
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion \(U.\iota\) from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to \(\mathrm{Over}\)-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on \(X\) and \(Y\) are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-\(\bar k\) argument (for \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}\) hitting the identity at a \(\bar k\)-point) to this theorem with \(X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(Y := A_{\bar k}\). The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~REF (the Albanese property of \(\Jac(C)\)): given two morphisms \(g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A\) with \(g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P\), scheme-level agreement on the image of \(\alpha_P\) (a non-empty open after passing to \(C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)\)) forces \(g_1 = g_2\).
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of REF requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\)-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66	
67	An upstream-PR candidate is a thin corollary in Mathlib's \texttt{Separated} file:
68	\[
69	  \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnNonemptyOpen} \;\colon\; \texttt{(non-empty open) + (reduced source) + (separated target) }\Rightarrow\texttt{ ext}
70	\]
71	packaging the dense-and-dominant chain into a single API call. The incremental value over the existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} is small (just the API convenience of "non-empty open" over "dominant morphism"); see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md} for the proposed statement and home file. The project does not pursue this upstream PR as part of the iter-125 work.
72

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}
41	
42	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
43	\begin{itemize}
44	  \item \(g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})\) (Serre duality).
45	  \item \(g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)\), where \(\chi\) is the Euler characteristic.
46	  \item For a smooth projective curve over \(\mathbb C\), \(g(C)\) equals the topological genus of \(C(\mathbb C)\).
47	\end{itemize}
48	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
49	
50	\section{Mathlib gap}
51	\label{sec:Genus_status}
52	
53	The honest definition \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) requires a \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of \(\struct C\) on the topology of opens of the underlying space of \(C\). Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
54	
55	\begin{itemize}
56	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\AddCommGrpCat\)-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)\).
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel \(\Module k\)-valued construction with unit object \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\) instead of \(\ULift\,\mathbb Z\), gluing through the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a \(\Module k\) structure on the cohomology groups.
58	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaf} (this project): for \(C\) over \(\Spec k\), the sheafification of the presheaf \(U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))\) where the \(k\)-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
59	  \item \textbf{\(H^0\) algebraic bridge} (this project): \(H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)\), via Mathlib's \(\Ext\) linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content \(H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k\) on a connected proper \(k\)-curve.
60	\end{itemize}
61	
62	The remaining Mathlib gap for the \emph{theorem} (as opposed to the \emph{definition}) of genus:
63	
64	\begin{itemize}
65	  \item \textbf{Serre finiteness for \(H^i(C, \mathcal F)\) on a proper \(k\)-curve.} Mathlib's \(\ModuleCat.\mathtt{finite\_ext}\) provides \(\Module.\mathtt{Finite}\) for module-Ext only, not sheaf-Ext. The Čech-cohomology scaffold is the recommended direction.
66	\end{itemize}
67	
68	\section{User authorisation of \texttt{noncomputable}}
69	\label{sec:Genus_noncomputable}
70	
71	The original challenge file writes \(\mathtt{def\ genus}\), not \(\mathtt{noncomputable\ def\ genus}\). Because \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\), \(\Abelian.\Ext.\mathtt{instModule}\), and the structure-sheaf-as-\(k\)-module construction are all noncomputable in Mathlib, the honest closure of the body requires the \(\mathtt{noncomputable}\) modifier on the \(\mathtt{def}\). The user explicitly authorised agents to add this modifier as a non-signature change: names, argument types, and argument order remain verbatim.
72	
73	\section{What this iteration does and does not}
74	
75	This iteration \emph{defines} the genus. It does not \emph{compute} it on any specific curve, and it does not prove that the result equals the geometric genus in the classical sense (which requires Serre finiteness for non-vacuity and Riemann--Roch for verification on examples). The downstream theorem \(\mathtt{smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}\) in \(\mathtt{Jacobian.lean}\) states that the Jacobian of \(C\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\) --- that theorem will require Serre finiteness on the way and is queued for the iteration that closes the Jacobian construction itself.
76

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.a sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem, the technical heart that
8	% turns the absolute Quot-functor representability problem on a projective
9	% morphism \(X \to S\) with coherent input \(\F\) into a finite collection of
10	% locally-closed strata over which \(\F\) becomes flat. Mathlib (master
11	% \texttt{b80f227}) has neither a coherent-sheaf flatness predicate over a
12	% scheme base nor the flattening-stratification construction; this chapter
13	% is the prover-ready specification for the new file
14	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean} which a
15	% subsequent iter (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
16	%
17	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
18	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
19	
20	\section{Setup and motivation}
21	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
22	
23	The construction of the Quot scheme \(\Quot_{\F/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) in Nitsure's exposition
24	of Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S5) proceeds by
25	embedding the functor of points of \(\Quot\) into a Grassmannian via Castelnuovo--Mumford
26	regularity, then cutting out the locus of \emph{quotients with flat fibres of the
27	prescribed Hilbert polynomial} by a closed-subscheme condition.  Cutting out this
28	locus uses, as a primary technical input, the existence of the
29	\emph{flattening stratification} of a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on a projective
30	\(S\)-scheme: a finite collection of locally-closed subschemes of \(S\) on each of which
31	the restriction of \(\F\) becomes flat.
32	
33	This chapter packages the Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem as the
34	foundational A.2.a building block. The Quot-scheme construction itself (A.2.b),
35	the relative Picard functor (A.1.c), and the assembly of the Picard scheme
36	representability theorem on a smooth proper curve (A.2.c) live in separate sibling
37	chapters.
38	
39	We work throughout with a noetherian base scheme \(S\), a projective morphism
40	\(\pi : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module \(\F\). The conclusion of Nitsure's
41	theorem covers an arbitrary noetherian base with a projective \(X \to S\), which
42	specialises directly to the Route~A consumer setting where \(S = T\) is an arbitrary
43	\(k\)-scheme, \(X = C \times_k T\) for \(C\) a smooth proper curve over the base field
44	\(k\), and \(\F\) is a coherent quotient sheaf produced by the Grassmannian embedding
45	of the Quot functor (cf.\ \ below).
46	
47	\section{Coherent flatness over a base}
48	\label{sec:coh_flat}
49	
50	\begin{definition}
51	
52	\leanok
53	[Coherent sheaf flat over the base]
54	  \label{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat}
56	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(f : X \to S\) a morphism of finite type, and
57	  \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module.  We say \(\F\) is \emph{flat over \(S\)}
58	  (equivalently, \(\F\) is \emph{\(f\)-flat}, or \emph{\(S\)-flat}) if for every
59	  point \(x \in X\) the stalk \(\F_x\) is a flat \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module, where the
60	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module structure is given by the composite
61	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)} \to \OO_{X, x} \to \End(\F_x)\).
62	  \textit{Source: cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 00HB (flat modules); [Nitsure], \S4
63	  passim.}
64	  Equivalently (after pulling back to an affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) and
65	  an affine open \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) with \(\F|_V = \widetilde{M}\)
66	  for \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module), \(\F|_V\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat in the sense that \(M\) is a
67	  flat \(A\)-module (with the \(A\)-module structure coming from the ring map
68	  \(A \to B\)).
69	\end{definition}
70	
71	\begin{remark}
72	\label{rem:coh_flat_pullback}
73	  The condition is stable under base change: for any morphism \(g : S' \to S\),
74	  pulling back along the cartesian square
75	  \[
76	    \begin{tikzcd}
77	      X' \ar[r, "g'"] \ar[d, "f'"'] & X \ar[d, "f"] \\
78	      S' \ar[r, "g"]                & S
79	    \end{tikzcd}
80	  \]
81	  yields a coherent \(\OO_{X'}\)-module \(\F' := (g')^* \F\) which is \(S'\)-flat
82	  whenever \(\F\) is \(S\)-flat.  This is the routine cancellation of tensor-product
83	  associativity at the level of stalks. The converse fails in general.
84	\end{remark}
85	
86	\section{Generic flatness}
87	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
88	
89	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
90	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
91	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
92	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
93	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
94	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
95	non-zero element of the base ring.
96	
97	\begin{theorem}
98	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
99	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
100	  
101	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
102	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
103	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
104	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
105	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
106	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
107	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
108	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
109	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
110	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
111	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
112	\end{theorem}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
115	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
116	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
117	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
118	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
119	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
120	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
121	% there exists a finite filtration
122	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
123	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
124	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
125	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
126	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
127	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
128	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
129	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
130	\begin{proof}
131	  
132	  Following [Nitsure], \S4: pass to the fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) and
133	  set \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\), \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\), \(n := \dim \mathrm{supp}_{\Spec B_K} M_K\).
134	  Argue by induction on \(n\), with base case \(n = -1\) (i.e.\ \(M_K = 0\)): each
135	  generator of \(M\) is annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\); taking the
136	  product of finitely many annihilators yields \(f \in A\) with \(f M = 0\), hence
137	  \(M_f = 0\) is trivially free.  For \(n \ge 0\), a prime filtration of \(M\) as a
138	  finite \(B\)-module
139	  \[
140	    0 = M_0 \subset M_1 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M
141	  \]
142	  with successive quotients \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B / \mathfrak{p}_i\) reduces the
143	  problem (via the splicing fact: \(M_{f'f''}\) is \(A_{f'f''}\)-free when \(M'_{f'}\)
144	  and \(M''_{f''}\) are free) to the case \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) for a prime
145	  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset B\), then to the case \(B\) a domain and \(M = B\).
146	  Noether normalisation applied to \(B_K / K\) yields algebraically independent
147	  \(b_1, \dots, b_n \in B\) with \(B_K\) finite over \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); choosing
148	  \(g \ne 0\) to clear denominators of integrality equations, \(B_g\) is finite over
149	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Let \(m\) be the generic rank: the short exact sequence
150	  \[
151	    0 \to A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0
152	  \]
153	  has \(T\) a finite torsion module over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), so $\dim
154	  \mathrm{supp}_{B_K} (K \otimes_{A_g} T) < n$.  The inductive hypothesis
155	  yields \(h \ne 0\) in \(A\) with \(T_h\) free over \(A_{gh}\); set \(f := gh\).
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\begin{theorem}
159	
160	\leanok
161	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
162	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
163	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
164	  
165	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
166	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
169	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
170	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
171	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
172	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
173	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
174	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
175	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
176	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
177	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
178	\end{theorem}
179	
180	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
181	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
182	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
183	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
184	% Theorem~REF by restricting to a non-empty
185	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
186	\begin{proof}
187	  
188	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(\Spec A \subseteq S\) where \(A\) is a
189	  noetherian domain. The morphism \(p\) is of finite type, so locally on \(X\)
190	  above this affine \(\F\) is the sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) for \(M\) a finite module
191	  over a finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\). Theorem~REF
192	  produces \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\) on each such patch;
193	  shrinking to the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec A\) globalises by patching
194	  finitely many such opens (using noetherianity of \(X\) above the affine).
195	\end{proof}
